proposals.title: A Distributionally Robust Least Squares Method for Structural Equation Models with Correlated Measurement Errors
proposals.motivation: In covariance structure analysis, the standard assumption of independent measurement errors is often violated in practice, leading to biased parameter estimates and incorrect model fit conclusions. Pre-2026 methods for handling correlated errors, such as specifying them a priori, are highly subjective and can lead to model overfitting. A robust, data-driven method to identify and estimate such correlations is needed to improve the validity of inferences in fields like psychology and political science.
proposals.hypothesis: A distributionally robust estimator, minimizing a worst-case expectation of the least squares discrepancy over a set of plausible error correlation structures, will yield more consistent parameter estimates and accurate model fit tests than standard methods when the independence assumption is violated.
proposals.method: We will extend the Distributionally Robust Least Squares Method (DR-LSM) framework. The technical approach involves defining an ambiguity set for the error covariance matrix using a moment-based constraint (e.g., a bound on the Frobenius norm of off-diagonal blocks). The estimator will be formulated as a minimax optimization problem: minimizing the maximum expected discrepancy function. We will develop an iterative algorithm, combining gradient-based optimization for model parameters with a semidefinite programming sub-problem to find the worst-case error correlation matrix within the ambiguity set, ensuring computational tractability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, less subjective alternative to ad-hoc model modifications for correlated errors. It will enhance the robustness of model specification searches and fit evaluation, directly contributing to improved practice in SEM as highlighted in Bentler's recent work on model fit and specification tests. The method will be implemented in user-friendly software to facilitate adoption.
proposals.novelty: While robust SEM methods exist for non-normality, applying distributional robustness specifically to relax the core assumption of error independence is novel. The approach is non-obvious as it systematically explores a *set* of plausible error structures rather than testing or estimating a single, fixed pattern.

proposals.title: Power Analysis for Complex Structural Equation Models: A Distributionally Robust Monte Carlo Approach
proposals.motivation: Statistical power analysis for testing structural equation models (SEMs) remains underdeveloped, particularly for complex models with non-normal data. Pre-2026 practice often relies on oversimplified assumptions (e.g., exact multivariate normality) or computationally prohibitive methods, leading to inaccurate power estimates. This gap hinders rigorous model evaluation and adequate sample size planning in applied research.
proposals.hypothesis: A Monte Carlo power analysis framework, integrating robust estimators like Distributionally-Least-Squares-Mean (DLSM) and robust chi-square tests, will yield more accurate and computationally feasible power estimates for complex SEMs under realistic data conditions (e.g., non-normality, missing data).
proposals.method: The method extends Bentler's work on robust tests and DLSM. We will develop an algorithm that: (1) uses a flexible data generation process (e.g., based on Fleishman's power method or Vale-Maurelli transformation) to simulate non-normal data consistent with a specified population model; (2) employs robust estimators (DLSM, Satorra-Bentler scaled test) for model fitting on each simulated sample; (3) calculates the empirical rejection rate of the model test (e.g., robust chi-square) across thousands of replications to estimate power; and (4) provides a computationally efficient implementation by deriving analytic approximations for the non-centrality parameter of the robust test statistics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide applied researchers with a practical and theoretically sound tool for conducting power analysis for complex SEMs, moving beyond the limitations of normal-theory assumptions. It will directly enhance the planning and interpretation of SEM studies in fields like psychology, political science, and public health.
proposals.novelty: The integration of robust estimation and testing procedures into a Monte Carlo power analysis framework for SEM is novel, addressing the critical joint problem of model complexity and data non-normality that existing software (e.g., Satorra & Saris method extensions) does not fully solve.

proposals.title: Identification and Robust Estimation of Non-Recursive Structural Equation Models: Integrating Econometric Simultaneous Equation Methods into Covariance Structure Analysis
proposals.motivation: Non-recursive structural equation models (SEMs) with feedback loops are common in social and behavioral sciences but present significant challenges for identification and consistent estimation. Pre-2026 SEM practice, including Bentler's work on distributionally-weighted least squares (DWLS), often focuses on recursive systems. Econometrics has a mature literature on identification (order, rank conditions) and estimation (instrumental variables, two-stage least squares) for simultaneous equations, but these methods are not fully integrated into mainstream SEM software and model evaluation frameworks.
proposals.hypothesis: Formally applying econometric simultaneous equation identification rules to non-recursive SEMs will clarify model specification, and integrating instrumental variable estimation with robust, distributionally-insensitive fitting functions (like DWLS) will yield more consistent parameter estimates and reliable model tests under realistic data conditions.
proposals.method: The research will develop a two-stage methodological framework. First, we will implement algorithmic checks for the order and rank conditions for identification within an SEM context, extending Bentler's EQS program architecture. Second, we will develop and test a new estimator that combines a first-stage instrumental variable (2SLS) approach with a second-stage robust minimum distance estimator (e.g., DWLS or GLS) on the fitted moment conditions. This hybrid estimator will be compared to standard maximum likelihood and existing robust estimators via Monte Carlo simulation, assessing bias, efficiency, and Type I error rates of associated chi-square tests under non-normality and specification error.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide SEM practitioners with clear, software-implementable guidelines for specifying identifiable non-recursive models. It will also deliver a new, robust estimator that mitigates the simultaneity bias inherent in standard ML estimation of such models, thereby enhancing the validity of substantive conclusions in fields like political science and climate research where feedback mechanisms are theorized.
proposals.novelty: While simultaneous equation methods exist in econometrics, their systematic integration with the robust, covariance-structure-based estimation and fit testing paradigm central to Bentler's pre-2026 work is novel. The proposed hybrid estimator specifically targets the weaknesses of ML for simultaneous equations under non-normal data, a gap not addressed by classic econometric or standard SEM approaches.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Least Squares Approach to Structural Equation Modeling: Estimation, Model Comparison, and Robustness
proposals.motivation: Traditional covariance structure analysis relies heavily on frequentist maximum likelihood and least squares estimation, with model fit assessed via chi-square tests and related indices. These methods can struggle with complex models, small samples, or non-normal data, and provide limited direct probabilistic interpretation for parameters and model comparisons. Integrating Bayesian inference offers a principled framework for incorporating prior knowledge, quantifying uncertainty via credible intervals, and comparing models using Bayes factors, addressing key limitations in current SEM practice.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian framework implemented through a least squares discrepancy function (e.g., a Bayesian adaptation of DLSM) will yield stable parameter estimates with valid uncertainty intervals under small samples and model misspecification, and Bayes factors derived from this framework will provide a more informative tool for model selection than traditional chi-square difference tests.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian estimator by treating the generalized least squares (GLS) discrepancy function as a negative log-likelihood, enabling the construction of a posterior distribution for model parameters. We will employ Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods, specifically Hamiltonian Monte Carlo via Stan, for sampling. Model comparison will utilize bridge sampling for accurate marginal likelihood computation to calculate Bayes Factors. The method's performance will be evaluated against traditional ML and DLSM in Monte Carlo simulations varying sample size, distribution, and degree of misspecification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fully operational Bayesian least squares SEM framework, extending Bentler's contributions to robust estimation. It will deliver new software routines (e.g., for R or Mplus) and practical guidelines for specifying priors and interpreting Bayes factors in applied research, enhancing the toolkit for political science and other fields using complex structural models.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian SEM exists, a direct Bayesian formulation of the least squares discrepancy family (like DLSM) for estimation and model comparison is novel. This approach uniquely bridges Bentler's robust frequentist methods with Bayesian inference, offering a hybrid that leverages the computational stability of least squares with the probabilistic advantages of Bayes.

proposals.title: A Distributionally-Limited Least Squares Method (DLSM) for Causal Modeling with Very Small Samples: Theory, Implementation, and Robustness
proposals.motivation: Causal modeling techniques, such as structural equation modeling (SEM), rely heavily on large-sample theory for inference, making them unreliable or inapplicable in fields like rare disease research, small-scale interventions, or pilot studies where sample sizes are very small (N < 30). Pre-2026, standard fit indices and chi-square tests are known to be severely biased under these conditions, leading to invalid conclusions. There is a critical need for methods that provide valid parameter estimates and trustworthy model evaluation when data is extremely limited.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a modified Distributionally-Limited Least Squares Method (DLSM), extended from its use in covariance modeling, can be adapted to provide consistent and less biased parameter estimates for causal models under very small sample sizes by directly incorporating distributional assumptions and robust error corrections into the fitting function.
proposals.method: The core approach will extend the DLSM framework to SEM. We will develop a new fitting function that penalizes the standard least squares discrepancy based on sample size and leverages empirical moment constraints. The method will integrate a scaled and adjusted chi-square test, building on Bentler's prior work on robust LM tests, to evaluate model fit. We will implement a bootstrap-assisted estimator correction procedure, specifically designed for N < 30, to compute standard errors and confidence intervals, comparing its performance against traditional maximum likelihood and asymptotic distribution-free estimators via Monte Carlo simulation.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a practical, theoretically-grounded tool for researchers conducting causal analyses with very small samples, filling a significant methodological gap. It will contribute a new software module for existing SEM packages and offer clear guidelines for model specification and testing in data-scarce contexts, enhancing the rigor of studies in resource-limited fields.
proposals.novelty: While small-sample corrections exist, adapting the DLSM—a method focused on distributional limits—specifically for causal parameter estimation and inference in SEM is novel. The integration of a principled, sample-size-aware fitting function with a robustified test statistic represents a non-obvious synthesis of Bentler's work on least squares, covariance modeling, and fit evaluation.

proposals.title: Scalable Fitting of High-Dimensional Structural Equation Models via Distributed Least Squares and Sparse Regularization
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of large-scale datasets in fields like genomics, neuroimaging, and social networks demands structural equation models (SEM) with hundreds of observed and latent variables. Pre-2026 standard SEM software, relying on inverting full covariance matrices and traditional maximum likelihood, becomes computationally intractable or fails entirely at this scale. This limits the application of rigorous latent variable modeling to modern 'big data' problems.
proposals.hypothesis: That a novel estimation framework combining distributed least squares methods (DLSM) with adaptive sparse regularization can efficiently and consistently fit high-dimensional structural equation models, overcoming the computational and identification barriers of traditional approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-stage algorithm. First, we will extend Bentler's DLSM for efficient, initial consistent estimation without full matrix inversion by partitioning variables. Second, we will integrate a group-lasso or adaptive lasso penalty on factor loadings and structural coefficients within the least squares loss to induce sparsity and ensure model identification. Third, a block-coordinate descent algorithm will be implemented, allowing distributed computation across variable blocks. Model fit will be assessed using robust, scaled chi-square statistics and fit indices adapted for regularized estimators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computationally feasible and statistically principled SEM framework for massive variable sets, enabling new applications in high-dimensional data analysis. It will contribute new theory for the properties of regularized least squares estimators in SEM and release open-source software modules to make large-scale SEM accessible.
proposals.novelty: While sparse SEM and distributed computing exist separately, their integration via a robust least squares loss function tailored for massive variable sets is novel. This approach directly addresses the simultaneous challenges of computation, identification, and model specification search in a unified algorithm, moving beyond incremental improvements to existing ML-based fitting functions.

proposals.title: A Regularized Least Squares Framework for Robust Estimation in Structural Equation Models with Categorical Indicators and Latent Interactions
proposals.motivation: Structural Equation Models (SEM) increasingly incorporate categorical variables and latent interaction terms to model complex social and behavioral phenomena. However, dummy coding and product term formation create high-dimensional, ill-conditioned covariance structures that violate the assumptions of standard estimation methods like Maximum Likelihood (ML) and even distributionally-robust methods like Regularized Least Squares (RLS). This leads to unstable, unreliable parameter estimates and poor model fit assessment, limiting the practical application of advanced SEM specifications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying targeted covariance matrix regularization (e.g., ridge, lasso, or eigenvalue shrinkage) within an RLS framework will stabilize estimation, yielding reliable parameter estimates and accurate model fit indices for complex SEMs with categorical indicators and latent interactions, even under conditions of severe multicollinearity and high dimensionality.
proposals.method: The research will develop a two-stage algorithm. First, we will generate the expanded covariance matrix of observed indicators, including dummy-coded categorical variables and product terms for latent interactions. Second, we will integrate a regularization penalty directly into the RLS fitting function, F_RLS = trace[(S - Σ(θ))^2] + λP(θ), where P(θ) is a penalty function (L1/L2 norm on parameter vectors or a direct ridge penalty on the covariance matrix eigenvalues). We will systematically compare the performance of ridge, lasso, and adaptive lasso penalties via Monte Carlo simulations, evaluating bias, efficiency, and Type I error rates of associated chi-square tests against benchmarks like ML and unregularized RLS.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, robust estimation framework for complex SEMs that are currently difficult to fit reliably, extending the utility of distributionally-robust methods. It will offer applied researchers concrete guidelines for selecting and tuning regularization parameters to obtain stable results with categorical data and interaction effects, enhancing the validity of conclusions in fields like political science and climate research where such models are essential.
proposals.novelty: While regularization is known in regression and some covariance estimation contexts, its direct integration into the RLS fitting function to specifically remedy the ill-conditioning induced by SEM model specification (rather than just small sample size) for categorical and interaction terms is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond using RLS as a simple robust alternative to ML, adapting it into a generalized tool for complex model architectures.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Pseudo-FIML Estimator for Covariance Structure Analysis with Ordinal Missing Data in Small Samples
proposals.motivation: In psychological and social science research, small-sample studies (n < 50) with ordinal data and missing values are common but pose severe challenges for covariance structure analysis (CSA). Standard Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML) estimation is unstable in such conditions and is sensitive to misspecification of the mean structure, which is often not of substantive interest in factor or network models. Pre-2026 methods like robust weighted least squares (WLSMV) require larger samples, and existing Bayesian CSA approaches typically estimate full mean and covariance structures, introducing unnecessary complexity and risk.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian-approximated FIML procedure that targets only the covariance structure, decoupling it from the mean model, will yield more stable and accurate parameter estimates and model fit assessments for CSA with ordinal missing data in very small samples compared to existing FIML and limited-information methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a pseudo-likelihood framework where the mean parameters are profiled out or assigned non-informative priors, focusing the Bayesian machinery on the covariance structure. The estimator will employ Integrated Nested Laplace Approximations (INLA) for fast, deterministic posterior approximation of polychoric/polyserial correlations and the structural model parameters under missing data. This approach regularizes the problematic thresholds and latent correlations in tiny samples. We will validate the method via Monte Carlo simulation against standard FIML and diagonally weighted least squares (DWLS), examining bias, efficiency, and Type I error rates of chi-square tests across sample sizes from 20 to 100.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, accessible estimation option for researchers conducting factor analysis or network modeling with limited ordinal data. It directly extends Bentler's work on robust model testing and fit evaluation by stabilizing the foundational estimation step in small-N, incomplete-data scenarios. The method will be implemented in user-friendly R code, integrating with existing SEM software ecosystems.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of a covariance-focused (mean-agnostic) pseudo-likelihood with modern Bayesian approximation techniques for small-sample ordinal missing data is novel. It strategically bypasses the contamination from mean structure misspecification, a problem not addressed by pre-2026 Bayesian SEM packages which estimate full parameter vectors.

proposals.title: A Robust Limited-Information Estimator for Covariance Structure Analysis: A Distributionally Agnostic Alternative to Mean-Inclusive Modeling
proposals.motivation: In covariance structure analysis (CSA), researchers often focus solely on modeling relationships among variables, with the mean structure being a nuisance parameter. Standard full-information methods like maximum likelihood (ML) require correct specification of both covariance and mean structures for valid inference, which is often unnecessary and can lead to inefficiency or bias if the mean model is misspecified. A robust, limited-information estimator focused exclusively on the covariance structure is needed to simplify model specification and improve robustness in common CSA applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A robust least squares (RLS) variant, derived from the distributionally agnostic principles of the Direct Least Squares Method (DLSM) but applied solely to the covariance structure, will yield parameter estimates and standard errors that are as efficient as, and more robust than, standard mean-inclusive estimators when the mean structure is of no substantive interest or is potentially misspecified.
proposals.method: We will develop a Covariance-Focused Robust Least Squares (CF-RLS) estimator. The method minimizes a weighted quadratic form of the discrepancies between the sample and model-implied covariance matrices, using an asymptotically distribution-free weight matrix. The core innovation is the derivation of its asymptotic covariance matrix using only the fourth-order moments of the data, explicitly excluding any terms related to the sample means or mean structure misspecification. We will implement this estimator and compare its performance in Monte Carlo simulations against standard ML and existing RLS methods under conditions of correct and incorrect mean specification, varying distributional forms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide applied researchers with a principled, easy-to-implement estimator for CSA that eliminates the need to model means when they are not theoretically relevant. It will extend the toolkit of robust, limited-information methods, offering greater specification robustness. The resulting software and guidelines will directly enhance practice in fields like political science and environmental modeling, where covariance structures are primary.
proposals.novelty: While RLS and DLSM exist, they typically operate on both mean and covariance moments. This proposal explicitly decouples from the mean structure, creating a purely covariance-focused RLS variant. This targeted approach is novel compared to the general robust estimators in Bentler's prior work and directly challenges the default inclusion of mean structures in standard SEM software.

proposals.title: A Comparative Decision Framework for Model Misspecification Diagnostics: Evaluating RLS and Alternative Indicators in Small-Sample Covariance Structure Analysis
proposals.motivation: Applied researchers in social and behavioral sciences frequently rely on covariance structure models with small samples, where model misspecification can severely bias parameter estimates and substantive conclusions. While the Ramsey Regression Equation Specification Error Test (RLS) is a known diagnostic, its relative performance against a suite of practical alternatives (e.g., Lagrange Multiplier tests, residual plots, influence statistics) under small-n conditions is not systematically understood. This leaves practitioners without clear, evidence-based guidance on which diagnostic tools are most informative for detecting specific types of misspecification, such as omitted nonlinearities or heteroskedasticity, when data are limited.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that no single diagnostic indicator is uniformly most powerful; instead, the relative diagnostic power of RLS compared to graphical methods and competing tests depends critically on sample size, the type of misspecification, and model complexity. A curated combination of procedures will provide a more robust and actionable assessment of model fit in small samples.
proposals.method: We will conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation study grounded in covariance structure analysis. Data will be generated from known population models with systematically introduced misspecifications (omitted quadratic terms, omitted variables, heteroskedastic errors). For each condition (varying n=50 to 200), we will fit the misspecified model and compute a set of diagnostics: the RLS test (applied to the implied regression equations), Lagrange Multiplier (LM) tests for specific paths, standardized residual plots, and influence metrics like Cook's D. Performance will be evaluated using empirical power (sensitivity) and Type I error rates (false alarms). The analysis will employ robust estimation methods (e.g., DLSM) to align with Bentler's recent work on robust specification searches.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a practical, evidence-based decision framework that recommends specific diagnostic workflows for researchers facing common small-sample constraints. It will directly extend Bentler's work on enhancing model fit evaluation by moving from optimizing single tests to optimizing diagnostic strategy. The framework will transform a known methodological limitation into actionable advice, improving the reliability of substantive research using structural equation modeling.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined the absolute power of individual tests like RLS, this study is novel in its comparative, decision-focused approach, directly pitting RLS against a curated set of alternatives under realistic small-sample conditions to generate a practical selection guideline. It integrates the LM test framework for specification searches with broader diagnostic evaluation in a unified simulation design.

proposals.title: A Robustness-Weighted Regularized Least Squares Estimator for Sensitivity Analysis in Small-Sample SEM with Incomplete Data
proposals.motivation: In small-sample SEM, the common assumption of Missing at Random (MAR) is both statistically precarious and empirically untestable, yet conclusions are often reported as if this single assumption holds. This creates a significant risk of biased inferences in fields like political science or environmental research, where sample sizes can be limited and missingness mechanisms are complex. Current methods lack a principled, computationally tractable framework to formally assess how conclusions depend on deviations from MAR in small-n contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding a class of plausible Missing Not at Random (MNAR) mechanisms within a Regularized Least Squares (RLS) estimation framework, it is possible to generate a robustness profile of parameter estimates and model fit indices, thereby quantifying the sensitivity of substantive conclusions to untestable missing data assumptions in small samples.
proposals.method: We will extend the RLS estimator to incorporate a sensitivity parameter, δ, which systematically perturbs the implied moment conditions to represent a pre-specified class of MNAR mechanisms (e.g., selection models). For a grid of δ values, we will compute the corresponding RLS estimates, standard errors (using a small-sample adjusted bootstrap), and model fit statistics (e.g., robustified chi-square tests). The core output will be a 'robustness-weighted' ensemble of results, where weights are assigned based on a plausible prior distribution for δ, culminating in a stability interval for key parameters and a diagnostic plot of model fit degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide applied researchers with a practical, transparent tool for conducting formal sensitivity analysis for missing data in small-sample SEM, moving beyond a single MAR-based model. It will advance methodological practice by replacing a binary 'MAR vs. not' decision with a continuous assessment of result stability, directly addressing the replication crisis in fields reliant on observational data with imperfections.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analysis for MNAR exists in biostatistics, its integration into the RLS-SEM framework for small samples is novel. The proposed method uniquely combines Bentler's expertise in covariance structure analysis, robust testing, and regularized estimation to produce a weighted ensemble of results, not just a point estimate under a single strong assumption.

proposals.title: Robustness of Regularized Least Squares (RLS) Estimation in Covariance Structure Analysis Under Violations of Normality: A Monte Carlo Simulation Study
proposals.motivation: In covariance structure analysis, Regularized Least Squares (RLS) estimators are increasingly used for their computational stability and ability to handle complex models. However, their statistical properties, particularly under the non-normal data conditions common in applied social and behavioral sciences, are not well understood. A systematic evaluation of RLS performance under distributional misspecification is crucial for providing practical guidance to researchers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the bias, efficiency, and Type I error rates of RLS parameter estimates and associated model fit tests will degrade under conditions of increasing multivariate non-normality, but that this degradation will be less severe for RLS compared to standard least squares estimators due to its inherent regularization.
proposals.method: We will conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation manipulating factors including sample size, model complexity, degree of multivariate kurtosis/skewness, and regularization parameter (λ). Data will be generated from specified population models using pre-2026 methods (e.g., Vale & Maurelli's approach). The performance of RLS will be assessed against benchmarks like Normal-Theory Maximum Likelihood (ML) and Diagonally Weighted Least Squares (DWLS) using metrics such as relative bias, mean square error, and empirical rejection rates for the chi-square test of model fit. The simulation design will extend established frameworks from Bentler's prior work on robust tests and model fit evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, simulation-based guidelines for the application of RLS under non-ideal data conditions. It will directly inform methodological practice in SEM by clarifying when RLS offers a robust alternative and will contribute to the literature on regularized estimation in psychometrics.
proposals.novelty: While simulation studies of estimators under non-normality exist, none have focused specifically on the Regularized Least Squares approach within the SEM framework. This work integrates Bentler's long-standing interest in robust covariance structure analysis with contemporary regularization methods.

proposals.title: A Practical Decision Framework for Estimator Selection in Complex SEM: A Multi-Criteria Benchmark of Robust Least Squares
proposals.motivation: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) is widely used in social and behavioral sciences, but applied researchers face a critical and often ambiguous choice when selecting an estimator for models with categorical variables or interactions. While robust alternatives like MLR, WLSMV, and bootstrap methods exist, and Robust Least Squares (RLS) has been proposed, there is a lack of clear, empirically grounded guidance on which estimator performs best under specific, realistic data conditions. This gap leads to suboptimal model fitting, unreliable inference, and inconsistent practice across studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that no single estimator (RLS, MLR, WLSMV, or bootstrap-adjusted) is universally superior, but that the optimal choice depends systematically on specific data characteristics (e.g., degree of non-normality, type of categorical variable, model complexity, and sample size).
proposals.method: We will conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation manipulating key data conditions: distribution (normal, skewed, heavy-tailed), measurement level (continuous, ordinal with varying categories), model complexity (with and without latent interactions), and sample size (small to large). For each condition, we will benchmark RLS against MLR, WLSMV, and a bootstrap-corrected ML estimator, evaluating them on a multi-criteria set of outcomes: parameter bias and efficiency, Type I and II error rates for model tests (e.g., chi-square, robust variants), computational speed, and implementation complexity in standard software (e.g., Mplus, lavaan). The simulation results will be synthesized using decision-tree and cost-benefit analysis to create a structured selection framework.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a practical, evidence-based decision framework that moves beyond simple performance tables to actionable guidelines for applied researchers. It will directly address the 'so what?' question by translating simulation findings into clear recommendations based on a study's specific data profile and inferential goals, thereby improving the robustness and reproducibility of SEM applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares estimator performance descriptively, this study innovates by developing a formal, multi-criteria decision framework that explicitly trades off statistical accuracy with practical usability, and by providing a focused benchmark of RLS—a method rooted in Bentler's work on least squares and covariance modeling—against the current robust standard in complex SEM scenarios.

proposals.title: Evaluating Robust Least Squares Estimation for Structural Equation Models with Categorical Indicators and Latent Interactions
proposals.motivation: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) increasingly addresses complex phenomena using categorical variables and latent interaction terms. Pre-2026, standard estimators like Maximum Likelihood (ML) can be biased with categorical data, while interaction models often rely on product-indicator or latent moderated structural (LMS) approaches that are computationally intensive and assume normality. Robust Least Squares (RLS), known for its efficiency with non-normal continuous data, has not been systematically evaluated for these more complex, yet common, model specifications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that RLS estimation, particularly the Direct Least Squares (DLSM) variant, will provide superior parameter estimates and model fit indices compared to standard estimators (e.g., ML, WLSMV) in SEMs with categorical indicators and latent interactions, especially under conditions of non-normality and moderate sample sizes.
proposals.method: We will conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation study. Data will be generated for models with categorical indicators (ordinal, binary) and latent interaction terms using established pre-2026 methods (e.g., copula approaches for non-normal latent variables). We will compare the performance of RLS/DLSM against ML, Robust ML, and Weighted Least Squares Mean and Variance adjusted (WLSMV). Performance metrics will include bias, efficiency, and Type I error rates of chi-square-based fit tests. The analysis will be implemented using the `lavaan` package in R, extending its capabilities to integrate RLS fitting functions for these complex models.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide clear, evidence-based guidelines for applied researchers on when and how to use RLS for complex SEMs, potentially offering a more robust and computationally efficient alternative. It will extend the theoretical understanding of RLS properties beyond standard continuous-variable models, contributing to the toolbox for robust covariance structure analysis.
proposals.novelty: While RLS is established for continuous data, its application and evaluation for models combining categorical indicators and latent interactions is novel. This bridges Bentler's long-standing work on robust covariance structure analysis with contemporary modeling challenges that have been addressed primarily with different estimator families.

proposals.title: A Unified Framework for Small-Sample Inference in Covariance Structure Analysis: Robust Standard Errors for Regularized Likelihood Shrinkage and a Comparative Calibration Study
proposals.motivation: In covariance structure analysis, small-sample inference remains a critical challenge. While methods like scaled chi-square tests (Satorra-Bentler), bootstrap, and newer Regularized Likelihood Shrinkage (RLS) exist, there is no principled framework for selecting the optimal method. Practitioners lack guidance on which technique provides accurate parameter standard errors and test statistics under specific conditions of sample size, model complexity, and data non-normality.
proposals.hypothesis: The newly derived robust standard error formulas for RLS estimators will provide a precise diagnostic tool, enabling a decomposition of total inference error into bias and variance components across methods. This will allow for the creation of condition-specific guidelines or an adaptive meta-procedure that selects the method with optimal coverage properties for a given analysis context.
proposals.method: We will first derive robust (sandwich-type) standard error estimators for RLS, extending pre-2026 work on normal theory and distributionally weighted least squares (DWLS). A comprehensive Monte Carlo simulation will benchmark RLS against Maximum Likelihood with Satorra-Bentler correction and nonparametric bootstrap. The simulation design will systematically vary sample size, model size, and distributional conditions. The robust RLS standard errors will be used to analytically decompose mean squared error of parameter estimates, isolating bias from variance for each method.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide: 1) The first robust standard error estimator for RLS, enhancing its practical utility. 2) A definitive, empirically grounded calibration study that maps methodological performance to data conditions. 3) Actionable guidelines or a prototype adaptive algorithm for applied researchers to achieve more reliable small-sample inferences in structural equation modeling.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of robust standard error development for a regularized estimator with a diagnostic-driven comparative framework is novel. Instead of a simple performance ranking, we use the new formulas to decompose error sources, moving towards an adaptive, condition-specific inference system rather than a one-size-fits-all recommendation.

proposals.title: Robust Standard Errors for Regularized Least Squares (RLS) Estimators in Small-Sample Covariance Structure Analysis
proposals.motivation: Regularized Least Squares (RLS) estimators are increasingly used for covariance structure analysis to handle model misspecification and improve predictive accuracy, especially in small samples. However, pre-2026 methodological work has focused primarily on point estimation, leaving a critical gap in inference: existing standard error formulas, often based on asymptotic normality, are known to be inaccurate in small samples, leading to unreliable hypothesis tests and confidence intervals. This undermines the practical utility of RLS in fields like political science and climate modeling, where robust inference from limited data is essential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sandwich-type robust standard error estimator, derived from the explicit RLS objective function and its influence function, will provide accurate coverage rates for RLS parameter estimates in finite samples, even under conditions of mild model misspecification and non-normality.
proposals.method: We will derive the theoretical covariance matrix for RLS estimates by extending the Generalized Least Squares (GLS) framework to include a differentiable penalty term (e.g., L2 or ridge). The core method involves: 1) formulating the sandwich estimator (A^{-1}BA^{-1}) where A is the Hessian of the penalized fit function and B is the covariance matrix of the penalized estimating equations, 2) implementing this estimator in software (e.g., R/Python) integrated with existing DLSM/RLS routines, and 3) validating performance via extensive Monte Carlo simulations. Simulations will vary sample size (n < 200), model size, degree of misspecification, and error distributions to compare the proposed robust SEs against naive asymptotic and bootstrap SEs.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first rigorously derived and empirically validated robust standard error formulas for RLS in SEM, directly enhancing the reliability of inference in small-sample studies. It will contribute a practical software implementation, allowing researchers to report accurate confidence intervals and conduct valid Wald tests for regularized parameters. This bridges the gap between modern regularization techniques and foundational inferential needs in Bentler's tradition of covariance structure analysis.
proposals.novelty: While robust standard errors are well-established for classic estimators like ML and GLS, their derivation for penalized/RLS estimators in SEM is non-trivial due to the bias-variance trade-off introduced by regularization. The novelty lies in formally adapting the sandwich estimator framework to the penalized objective function and systematically evaluating its finite-sample properties, an area largely unexplored in pre-2026 SEM literature.

proposals.title: Conditional Performance of Small-Sample Corrections to Maximum Likelihood Fit Statistics: A Theory-Driven Simulation Framework for Selecting Robust Methods
proposals.motivation: Maximum Likelihood (ML) estimation in covariance structure analysis relies on large-sample theory, leading to biased chi-square statistics and standard errors in small samples. Pre-2026 corrections like Satorra-Bentler scaling and bootstrap exist, but their relative performance is inconsistent across studies, leaving applied researchers without clear, conditional guidance. This ambiguity undermines model evaluation and specification searches, particularly in fields with inherent sample size limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: The performance of small-sample corrections (Regularized Likelihood Scaling, scaled chi-square, and bootstrap) is systematically predictable based on a combination of sample size (N), number of indicators (p), degree of non-normality, and model misspecification level. No single method will be uniformly superior; optimal choice depends on the specific analytical context.
proposals.method: We will implement a comprehensive Monte Carlo simulation manipulating four factors: N (50-400), p (10-40), distribution (normal, moderate/severe kurtosis), and misspecification (correct specification vs. omitted cross-loadings/paths). Performance of ML, RLS, Satorra-Bentler scaled/adjusted tests, and Bollen-Stine bootstrap will be evaluated on bias in chi-square, Type I error rate, and power. Analysis will use factorial ANOVA and decision-tree algorithms to model how correction performance varies with the manipulated conditions, generating a predictive framework.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a set of empirically grounded, conditional guidelines (e.g., decision charts) for selecting the most robust small-sample correction given a study's specific characteristics. It will advance methodological practice by moving beyond generic recommendations to a theory-informed mapping of correction performance, directly enhancing the reliability of model fit evaluation in small-N research.
proposals.novelty: While simulations of individual corrections exist, this study is novel in its systematic, theory-driven design to predict *differential* performance and generate actionable selection rules. It integrates Bentler's recent work on robust LM tests and model fit evaluation into a unified framework for small-sample inference.

proposals.title: A Robust M-Estimation Framework for Measurement Invariance Testing with Ordinal Data Under Joint Model and Data Mechanism Misspecification
proposals.motivation: Testing measurement invariance is crucial for valid group comparisons in SEM, but conclusions are threatened by multiple, co-occurring issues. Pre-2026 literature treats outliers, model misspecification (e.g., incorrect thresholds or loadings), and missing data (MNAR) as separate problems. In practice, they interact, leading researchers to incorrectly attribute misfit to invariance violations, risking false substantive conclusions.
proposals.hypothesis: Robust M-estimators, using appropriate influence functions, can provide stable parameter estimates and reliable chi-square difference tests for invariance even under the joint presence of anomalous observations, a misspecified measurement model, and MNAR data, and a diagnostic decomposition of misfit can identify the primary source of invariance test failure.
proposals.method: We will extend Bentler's work on robust LM tests and DLS estimation. The procedure integrates a robust M-estimator (e.g., based on a Huber-type weight function) for polychoric correlations within a multiple-group ordinal SEM framework. We will develop a comprehensive simulation study manipulating levels of outlier contamination, factor structure misspecification, and MNAR mechanisms. The core diagnostic will be a decomposition of the robust test statistic (e.g., robust scaled chi-square difference) into components attributable to outliers, model error, and true group differences, using influence analysis and Lagrange Multiplier tests.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a unified, practical procedure for conducting more defensible invariance tests with real-world, messy ordinal data. It will offer applied researchers clear diagnostics to distinguish genuine population differences from artifacts of data and model problems, enhancing the reliability of cross-group comparisons in psychology, education, and health.
proposals.novelty: While robust estimators and invariance testing exist separately, their integration for ordinal data under *simultaneous* model misspecification and MNAR is novel. The diagnostic framework to disentangle the sources of misfit moves beyond simple fit evaluation to causal attribution of test failure.

proposals.title: Regularized FIML for High-Dimensional Ordinal Structural Equation Models with Missing Data: A Scalable RLS-Based Approach
proposals.motivation: Contemporary social and psychological research increasingly relies on large-scale surveys with many ordinal items (p >> n), where missing data is pervasive. Existing methods, such as diagonally weighted least squares (DWLS) or pairwise deletion, struggle with both high dimensionality and missingness, leading to unstable estimates and poor model selection. A unified, computationally efficient method is needed to enable the testing of complex theories with real-world, incomplete ordinal data.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating sparsity-inducing penalties directly into a Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML) framework for ordinal data will yield a regularized estimator that provides stable parameter recovery, accurate model selection, and valid inference in high-dimensional SEM with missing data, outperforming existing two-step or ad-hoc methods.
proposals.method: We will extend the underlying normal-theory FIML likelihood for ordinal data, modeled via underlying continuous variables, by incorporating L1 (lasso) and/or L2 (ridge) penalties on primary factor loadings and structural coefficients. Estimation will proceed via a scalable Regularized Least Squares (RLS) algorithm, optimizing a penalized fit function derived from the full-data likelihood. The approach will handle missing data inherently within the FIML framework, using an EM-type algorithm where the M-step involves solving the regularized optimization problem. Model fit will be evaluated using robust extensions of the chi-square test and information criteria (e.g., BIC) adapted for regularization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a statistically principled and computationally feasible tool for analyzing high-dimensional ordinal data with missing values, a common scenario in modern psychometrics and social science. It will advance SEM methodology by fusing regularization for dimension reduction with FIML for missing data, moving beyond current limited solutions. The resulting software implementation will empower researchers to fit more realistic models to complex datasets.
proposals.novelty: While regularization exists for continuous SEM and FIML is standard for missing data, their integration into a single framework specifically for high-dimensional ordinal data is novel. This proposal directly fuses Bentler's recent work on robust tests and scalable RLS estimation with the long-standing challenge of incomplete ordinal measurement.

proposals.title: A Scaled Regularized Least Squares Estimator for High-Dimensional Covariance Structure Analysis
proposals.motivation: In fields like genomics and neuroimaging, researchers increasingly face high-dimensional, low-sample-size (HDLSS) data where the number of variables (p) far exceeds the sample size (n). Standard covariance structure analysis (CSA) and structural equation modeling (SEM) methods, including normal-theory maximum likelihood and least squares, fail in this regime because the sample covariance matrix is singular and model fit tests are invalid. Pre-2026 work on regularized estimators often lacks a principled scaling for model fit evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel scaled Regularized Least Squares (RLS) estimator, incorporating a data-adaptive penalty and a scaling factor for the discrepancy function, will yield stable parameter estimates and a valid model test statistic for HDLSS SEM, even when p > n.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RLS framework by developing a two-stage estimator. First, we will derive a ridge-type penalty on model-implied covariance parameters, with the regularization parameter selected via cross-validation on the Frobenius norm. Second, we will scale the resulting RLS discrepancy function by a factor derived from the effective degrees of freedom of the regularized fit, analogous to scaling corrections for normal theory statistics. The performance of the scaled test statistic (T_scaled-RLS) will be evaluated via Monte Carlo simulation across varying p/n ratios and model complexities, comparing it to existing ad-hoc regularized methods and the Direct Least Squares Method (DLSM).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a rigorously defined, computationally feasible estimator for SEM in HDLSS contexts. It will deliver both stable parameter estimates and a statistically defensible model fit test, directly addressing a critical limitation in applying SEM to modern big data. The work will extend Bentler's contributions to robust model evaluation into the high-dimensional domain.
proposals.novelty: While regularization in SEM is not new, the explicit integration of a scaling factor for the test statistic within an RLS framework to correct for bias in HDLSS conditions is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond simple parameter shrinkage to provide a comprehensive solution for estimation and inference.

proposals.title: Robust N-of-1 Dynamic Modeling: A Regularized Least Squares SEM Framework with Person-Specific MNAR Sensitivity Analysis for Ordinal Intensive Longitudinal Data
proposals.motivation: Intensive longitudinal data (ILD) from single individuals (N-of-1) are crucial for personalized medicine and psychology, but standard methods for analyzing dynamic structures via SEM are ill-suited. They typically assume complete data or population-level missingness mechanisms, which is unrealistic for ILD where missingness (e.g., skipping a survey due to low mood) is often Not at Random (MNAR) and idiosyncratic to the individual. Pre-2026, there is a lack of principled, accessible tools for assessing the sensitivity of person-specific causal inferences to plausible MNAR mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: An adapted regularized least squares (RLS) estimator, embedded within a principled sensitivity analysis framework, can provide stable and interpretable estimates of person-specific dynamic networks from ordinal ILD, and the inferred network structure will be systematically sensitive to assumptions about the individual's unique MNAR mechanism.
proposals.method: We will extend the Distribution-Free Least Squares (DLS) approach, a precursor to Bentler's DLSM, for single-case ordinal data with an L1/L2 penalty for regularization (RLS). The core innovation is a sensitivity analysis protocol where the likelihood of missingness is explicitly modeled as a function of the latent variables (e.g., true mood) and/or observed outcomes. We will implement a computationally efficient algorithm that re-fits the N-of-1 SEM across a grid of sensitivity parameters defining the MNAR mechanism, using robust fit indices (e.g., scaled chi-square variants, SRMR) to evaluate stability. Simulation studies will evaluate RLS performance against diagonally weighted least squares (DWLS) under various MNAR scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical toolbox for conducting person-specific MNAR sensitivity analysis, moving beyond population-averaged 'one-size-fits-all' MNAR models. It will deliver concrete guidelines on how robust N-of-1 network inferences are likely to be for common ordinal ILD designs, enhancing the credibility of idiographic causal discovery.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of regularized least squares SEM for N-of-1 ordinal data with a formal, individualized MNAR sensitivity framework is novel. It shifts the focus from estimating a single population MNAR model to mapping how an individual's unique inference changes under different plausible missingness stories.

proposals.title: Extending Robust Least Squares Estimation to Mean and Covariance Structure Models with Categorical Indicators: A Polychoric Correlation Approach
proposals.motivation: Structural equation modeling (SEM) with categorical or ordinal observed variables is ubiquitous in social, behavioral, and health sciences. While robust least squares (RLS) methods, like the Direct Least Squares Method (DLSM), offer advantages for continuous data by minimizing sensitivity to distributional assumptions, their application to models with categorical indicators remains formally undeveloped. This gap limits the toolkit for researchers analyzing non-continuous data who seek estimators that are less reliant on strict normality assumptions compared to maximum likelihood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that RLS estimation for mean and covariance structures can be effectively adapted for categorical indicators by employing a two-stage procedure that first estimates a latent continuous variable correlation matrix (polychoric/polyserial) and then applies a suitably weighted RLS fit function to this matrix, yielding stable parameter estimates and reliable model tests.
proposals.method: The research will develop and evaluate a two-stage estimator. First, we will compute polychoric, polyserial, and Pearson correlation matrices from the raw categorical/ordinal data, forming an augmented sample moment matrix. Second, we will apply a robust weighted least squares fit function, extending Bentler's DLSM framework, to minimize the discrepancy between this sample moment matrix and the model-implied moment matrix. The weight matrix will be a robust inverse covariance matrix of the estimated polychoric correlations. We will derive the asymptotic properties of the estimator and implement a Monte Carlo simulation study to compare its performance (bias, efficiency, Type I error rates for the chi-square test) against established estimators like Weighted Least Squares (WLS) and Diagonally Weighted Least Squares (DWLS) under various conditions of sample size, category number, and distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally justified RLS-based estimation option for SEM with categorical data, expanding the methodological repertoire beyond ML and WLS variants. It will offer practical guidance on when this robust approach may be preferable, potentially providing better control of test statistics with smaller samples or more severe underlying distributions. The resulting algorithms and proofs will be made available for implementation in software like EQS.
proposals.novelty: While RLS is established for continuous data and polychoric correlations are standard for categorical data, their integration into a unified RLS framework for mean and covariance structure modeling is novel. This moves beyond simply applying existing categorical-data estimators to explicitly robustify the second-stage fitting process against the inherent variability in estimated polychoric correlations.

proposals.title: Robust Covariance Structure Estimation for Small Samples: Adapting M- and S-Estimators from Linear Regression
proposals.motivation: Covariance structure analysis, including structural equation modeling (SEM), is highly sensitive to distributional violations and outliers, especially in small samples where asymptotic normality assumptions fail. Pre-2026, robust methods like Satorra-Bentler scaling exist but primarily adjust test statistics, not the fundamental estimation of the covariance matrix itself. A method that directly yields robust covariance parameter estimates in small-N contexts is a critical unmet need for applied researchers.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the objective functions of M-estimators (for efficiency) and S-estimators (for high breakdown point) from robust linear regression to the context of covariance structure modeling will yield parameter estimates with less bias and greater efficiency than normal-theory maximum likelihood or distributionally weighted least squares (DWLS) in small, non-normal samples.
proposals.method: We will derive a robust fitting function by replacing the standard Gaussian discrepancy with a robust loss function, ρ, applied to the residuals of the observed versus model-implied covariance matrices. For M-estimation, we will implement an iteratively reweighted least squares algorithm, down-weighting influential data points. For S-estimation, we will minimize a robust measure of scale (e.g., an M-estimate of scale) of the residuals. The algorithms will be integrated into an existing SEM framework (e.g., EQS) and evaluated via Monte Carlo simulation comparing bias, efficiency, and Type I error rates against ML, DWLS, and robust test corrections under varied sample sizes and contamination conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, principled estimator for covariance structures that is inherently robust from the estimation stage, not just in testing. It will extend the toolkit of robust statistics into the domain of latent variable modeling, offering a more reliable alternative for small-sample studies in fields like psychology and political science where data often violate normality.
proposals.novelty: While robust *tests* in SEM are well-studied, directly applying the core machinery of M- and S-estimation to fit the covariance structure parameters is a novel synthesis. This approach moves beyond post-hoc corrections to the likelihood function itself, offering a fundamentally different estimation pathway tailored for small, messy data.

proposals.title: Robust Least Squares Estimation in High-Dimensional, Low-Sample-Size Covariance Structure Analysis: A Monte Carlo Simulation Study
proposals.motivation: The increasing prevalence of high-dimensional data (e.g., from genomics, neuroimaging, and large-scale surveys) presents a significant challenge for covariance structure analysis (CSA), where the number of variables (p) often approaches or exceeds sample size (N). Traditional maximum likelihood (ML) and standard least squares (LS) estimators, which assume N >> p, can become unstable, non-convergent, or exhibit severe bias under these conditions. There is a pressing need to evaluate and adapt robust estimation methods for the high-dimensional, low-sample-size (HDLSS) regime prevalent in modern research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Robust Least Squares (RLS) estimators, which downweight influential observations, will demonstrate superior performance—in terms of parameter bias, standard error accuracy, and convergence rates—compared to standard LS and diagonally weighted least squares (DWLS) when applied to high-dimensional structural equation models with small samples.
proposals.method: We will conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation study manipulating key factors: model dimensionality (p from 20 to 200), sample size (N from 50 to 500, creating p/N ratios from 0.1 to 4), distributional conditions (normal, skewed, heavy-tailed), and model misspecification. The core technical approach will implement RLS estimation using iteratively reweighted algorithms (e.g., Huber-type weights) to minimize a robust discrepancy function between the sample and model-implied covariance matrices. Performance will be benchmarked against standard LS, DWLS, and ML using pre-2026 fit indices (e.g., CFI, RMSEA) and the robust variants of the chi-square test of exact fit, a focus of Bentler's recent work.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide concrete, evidence-based guidelines for applied researchers facing HDLSS data, specifying when RLS offers a viable alternative. It will extend the theory of robust estimation in CSA to high-dimensional contexts and contribute new simulation code for the evaluation of estimators under these challenging conditions.
proposals.novelty: While RLS is established for low-dimensional problems, its systematic evaluation and potential advantage in the HDLSS regime—where both dimensionality and outliers threaten validity—is unexplored. This bridges Bentler's expertise in robust testing and estimation with the emergent practical challenge of high-dimensional modeling.

proposals.title: Determining the Viability Frontier: A Monte Carlo Study of Regularized Least Squares for Mean and Covariance Structures with Ultra-Small Samples
proposals.motivation: In specialized research areas (e.g., rare diseases, high-cost experiments), obtaining large samples is often impossible, forcing reliance on ultra-small samples (N < 30). Traditional covariance structure analysis methods like maximum likelihood are known to fail under such conditions. There is a critical need to establish the practical lower bounds for newer, more robust estimators like Regularized Least Squares (RLS), which has shown promise for model fit evaluation but whose limits are unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that RLS estimation, due to its inherent regularization properties, will remain viable for recovering mean and covariance structure parameters at sample sizes significantly smaller than the established minima for maximum likelihood, provided the model is sufficiently parsimonious.
proposals.method: We will conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation study manipulating sample size (from N=5 to N=50), model complexity (parsimonious vs. saturated pathways), and population discrepancy. Using a modified DLSM (Distributionally-weighted Least Squares with regularization) algorithm, we will generate and analyze covariance matrices. Viability will be assessed using pre-2026 metrics: parameter bias, standard error accuracy, and the robustness of the Satorra-Bentler scaled chi-square test under these extreme conditions, comparing RLS directly to ML and ULS.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide concrete, empirically derived guidelines for the minimum sample size required for RLS in SEM, filling a major gap in methodological literature. It will equip applied researchers in small-sample fields with a defensible, quantitative basis for using or avoiding covariance modeling.
proposals.novelty: While small-sample performance of estimators has been studied, a systematic investigation pushing into the 'ultra-small' regime (N < 20) specifically for Regularized Least Squares, and establishing an absolute 'viability frontier' for mean-and-covariance structures, is novel and addresses a pressing, unmet methodological challenge.

proposals.title: Robust Model Selection in Small Samples: Developing RLS-Based Information Criteria for Covariance Structure Analysis
proposals.motivation: In covariance structure analysis, model selection using information criteria (e.g., AIC, BIC) typically relies on normal-theory maximum likelihood (ML) fit functions, which are sensitive to distributional violations and perform poorly in small samples. Pre-2026 research has established robust least squares (RLS) methods, like DLSM, as effective for parameter estimation under non-normality, but their potential for model selection remains underexplored. This gap is critical for applied researchers in fields like political science and environmental modeling, who often work with small, non-normal datasets and require reliable model comparisons.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that information criteria (AIC, BIC) derived from robust least squares (RLS) fit functions will provide more accurate and stable model selection in small-sample and non-normal conditions than criteria based on standard maximum likelihood. Specifically, RLS-based criteria will better recover the true underlying model in simulation studies.
proposals.method: We will extend the theory of RLS estimation (e.g., DLSM, GLS) to define a robust fit statistic suitable for model selection. We will derive explicit formulas for RLS-based AIC and BIC, incorporating appropriate penalty terms for model complexity. The performance of these new criteria will be evaluated via extensive Monte Carlo simulations, varying sample size, distributional non-normality, and model misspecification. We will compare their performance against standard ML-based AIC/BIC and other robust alternatives (e.g., from Satorra-Bentler corrections) using metrics like model recovery rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, practical method for robust model selection in structural equation modeling, directly complementing existing RLS estimation techniques. It will offer applied researchers a more reliable tool for comparing competing theories with limited data, enhancing the validity of conclusions in fields like social and environmental sciences. The project will also extend the utility of Bentler's DLSM framework beyond estimation to the critical stage of model choice.
proposals.novelty: While RLS methods are established for robust estimation, their adaptation into formal information criteria for small-sample model selection is novel. This approach moves beyond using RLS fit values as simple heuristics, providing a rigorous, theoretically-grounded alternative to ML-based selection that is not merely a correction but a fundamentally different criterion built for robustness.

proposals.title: Robustness of Distributionally Insensitive Test Statistics to Model Under-Identification in Covariance Structure Analysis
proposals.motivation: In covariance structure analysis, standard model fit tests like the chi-square rely on the assumption of a correctly identified model. In practice, researchers often work with models that are locally under-identified or have empirically near-singular information matrices, especially in complex applications like pathway analysis. The performance of newer, distributionally insensitive test statistics (e.g., those based on robust or distributionally weighted least squares, DLSM) under such non-ideal identification conditions remains largely unexplored, posing a risk to their valid application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that certain distributionally insensitive fit statistics, particularly those derived from robust asymptotically distribution-free (ADF) or Satorra-Bentler scaled corrections, will demonstrate greater robustness to mild model under-identification compared to the standard normal-theory likelihood ratio test, maintaining more accurate Type I error rates and useful power properties.
proposals.method: We will conduct a comprehensive Monte Carlo simulation study. Data will be generated from population models with known parameters, and fitted using misspecified models that are systematically under-identified by imposing constraints that create local non-identification or near-singularity in the information matrix. We will evaluate and compare the empirical performance of several test statistics: the standard normal-theory chi-square, the Satorra-Bentler scaled statistic, and statistics from Diagonally Weighted Least Squares (DWLS) and Distributionally Weighted Least Squares (DLSM) estimators. Key performance metrics will include empirical Type I error rates, power to detect specific misspecifications, and bias in parameter estimates under the varied identification conditions.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide concrete, empirical guidelines for applied researchers on the conditions under which advanced fit statistics can be reliably used when model identification is uncertain. It will extend the theoretical understanding of robustness in structural equation modeling beyond distributional assumptions to include the critical condition of model identification, a common practical challenge.
proposals.novelty: While the robustness of test statistics to distributional violations is well-studied, their specific behavior under model under-identification—a distinct and common problem—is a novel and non-obvious line of inquiry that bridges identification theory with practical fit assessment.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Robustness of New Test Statistics for Overparameterized Structural Equation Models: A Monte Carlo Study
proposals.motivation: In covariance structure analysis, model fit is typically assessed under the assumption of correct or underparameterized models. However, applied researchers often face the risk of overparameterization, where models include unnecessary parameters, leading to potential overfitting and reduced generalizability. The performance of recently proposed test statistics (e.g., variants of the Satorra-Bentler scaled chi-square, robust residual-based tests) in this common but understudied scenario remains unknown, limiting their practical utility for model specification searches.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the four new robust test statistics, while effective for underparameterized models, will exhibit different Type I error and power profiles when models are overparameterized, potentially leading to over-rejection of correctly specified but overly complex models.
proposals.method: We will conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation study. Using the DLSM (Distributionally-Limited Satorra-Muthen) approach and other robust estimators, we will generate data from known population models and fit overparameterized models (e.g., adding extraneous paths or factors). We will systematically evaluate the performance of the four new statistics (e.g., scaled and adjusted variants) under varying conditions of sample size, degree of overparameterization, and distributional non-normality. The analysis will focus on empirical Type I error rates and power to detect true misspecification in the presence of redundant parameters.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first comprehensive guidelines for using modern fit statistics in the context of overparameterized SEM, a critical step for robust model specification searches. It will directly enhance the practical advice offered to applied researchers in fields like political science and environmental modeling, helping them avoid overfitting while maintaining valid model tests.
proposals.novelty: While the literature extensively examines test performance for underparameterized models, the systematic evaluation of these new statistics specifically for overparameterized models is a novel and non-obvious extension, addressing a key gap between methodological development and real-world modeling practice.

proposals.title: A Comparative Simulation Study of Model Modification Indices: Evaluating the Performance of New DLSM-Based Statistics Against Wald and Expected Parameter Change
proposals.motivation: In covariance structure analysis, researchers rely on modification indices (MIs) like the Lagrange Multiplier (LM) test to identify local model misspecifications. However, the performance of newer statistics, such as those derived from Distributionally-weighted Least Squares Methods (DLSM) or robust estimators, has not been systematically benchmarked against established alternatives like the Wald test or the Expected Parameter Change (EPC). A comprehensive comparison is needed to guide applied researchers in choosing the most reliable tool for model improvement, especially under non-normal data conditions prevalent in social and behavioral sciences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the new DLSM-based modification statistics will demonstrate superior robustness and accuracy in identifying true model misspecifications under conditions of non-normality and model complexity, compared to traditional LM, Wald, and EPC indices, which may be more sensitive to distributional assumptions.
proposals.method: We will conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation study manipulating factors such as sample size, degree of non-normality, model complexity, and type of misspecification (e.g., omitted cross-loadings or residual covariances). The performance of the new statistics (developed from Bentler's recent work on DLSM and robust tests) will be compared against standard LM, Wald, and EPC indices using criteria like true positive rate (sensitivity), false positive rate, and bias in the estimated parameter change. Analysis will utilize the EQS software framework for implementing DLSM estimators and calculating the associated modification indices.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a clear, evidence-based guideline for selecting modification indices in practice, particularly for models estimated with robust methods. It will extend the evaluation framework for model fit diagnostics beyond the chi-square test, directly addressing the practical tips highlighted in Bentler's recent work on enhancing SEM fit evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While individual modification indices are well-known, a systematic head-to-head performance comparison focusing on newer, robust DLSM-based statistics against the full suite of existing indices is novel. This moves beyond improving a single test (like the LM) to establishing a relative efficacy hierarchy for model specification searches.

proposals.title: Robustness of Distributionally-Least-Squares Methods in Covariance Structure Analysis Under Non-Normal Data Conditions
proposals.motivation: Structural equation modeling (SEM) often relies on maximum likelihood estimation and associated test statistics that assume multivariate normality. Real-world data in fields like psychology, political science, and climate research frequently violate this assumption, leading to biased standard errors, incorrect chi-square tests, and misleading model fit indices. Evaluating the robustness of newer, computationally efficient estimators like Distributionally-Least-Squares Methods (DLSM) under realistic distributional violations is therefore a critical, unresolved problem for applied researchers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the new DLSM-based test statistics will demonstrate superior robustness to non-normality (e.g., skewness, kurtosis, outliers) compared to traditional maximum-likelihood-based chi-square tests, maintaining better Type I error control and statistical power across a range of model misspecifications.
proposals.method: We will conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation study manipulating: (1) data distribution (multivariate normal, symmetric heavy-tailed, skewed, mixed normal-contaminated), (2) sample size (small to large), and (3) model misspecification level. The core technical approach will involve generating data from known population models, fitting models using DLSM and standard estimators, and comparing the performance of the associated fit statistics (e.g., chi-square, robust variants) against benchmarks. Performance will be evaluated based on empirical Type I error rates, statistical power, and bias in parameter estimates.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide clear, evidence-based guidelines for applied researchers on when DLSM estimators and their associated tests can be reliably used with non-normal data. It will extend the methodological literature on robust covariance structure analysis by systematically quantifying the limits of robustness for a promising class of least-squares-based methods.
proposals.novelty: While the asymptotic properties of DLSM are known under normality, its finite-sample performance and the behavior of its test statistics under systematic, severe distributional violations have not been comprehensively studied. This study uniquely integrates DLSM's estimation framework with a rigorous evaluation of its inferential tools under conditions mirroring real-world data problems.

proposals.title: A Distributionally Robust Lagrange Multiplier Test for Parameter Stability in Simple Regression Models
proposals.motivation: In applied social and environmental sciences, researchers often rely on simple regression models for forecasting and inference. Pre-2026, robust specification searches in complex covariance structure models (SEM) have been advanced, but analogous, easily accessible tools for foundational regression models are lacking. This gap is critical because misspecification due to parameter instability (e.g., structural breaks, heteroscedasticity) in these simpler models can lead to biased forecasts and invalid inferences in fields like climate economics or political polling.
proposals.hypothesis: The principles of robust Lagrange Multiplier (LM) tests for model specification in covariance structure analysis can be successfully adapted and simplified to create a powerful, distributionally robust test for parameter stability in ordinary least squares (OLS) and generalized linear regression models.
proposals.method: We will adapt the robust LM test framework from Bentler's recent work on SEM to the simpler regression context. The core method involves: (1) Deriving the score vector and information matrix under the null hypothesis of parameter stability for a standard regression model. (2) Incorporating a Distributionally-Loaded Structural Modeling (DLSM) approach to weight the score functions, making the test statistic robust to non-normal errors and heteroscedasticity. (3) Developing the asymptotic theory and a scaled chi-square test statistic suitable for simple regression.
