(4) Validating the test's size and power via Monte Carlo simulations against known alternatives like Chow tests and CUSUM tests under various error distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide applied researchers with a unified, robust diagnostic tool for regression specification searches, directly extending advanced SEM methodology to a more widely used modeling class. It will enhance the reliability of model-based forecasts and inferences in practice by offering a test that maintains correct size under non-ideal conditions (non-normality, heteroscedasticity) while having good power to detect instability.
proposals.novelty: While LM tests exist for regression, the novel contribution is the specific adaptation and simplification of the *robust* SEM-based LM test machinery—particularly the DLSM weighting for distributional robustness—to the simple regression setting, creating a new, practical test not previously available in standard statistical software.

proposals.title: A Sensitivity Analysis Framework for Multilevel Covariance Structure Models Using Distributionally Robust Least Squares
proposals.motivation: Multilevel structural equation models (MSEMs) are crucial for analyzing clustered data in social, behavioral, and health sciences. Pre-2026, model evaluation in MSEMs heavily relies on global fit indices and likelihood-based tests, which can be sensitive to distributional assumptions and offer limited insight into the stability and local sensitivity of specific parameter estimates. A method to systematically evaluate parameter sensitivity within the complex constraints of hierarchical covariance structures is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending a distributionally robust least squares (DRLS) estimation framework to MSEMs will yield a computationally tractable sensitivity statistic that can identify parameters most vulnerable to model misspecification and distributional violations across levels.
proposals.method: We will extend the Distributionally Robust Least Squares Method (DLSM) framework, previously applied to single-level models, to the two-level covariance structure. The core algorithm will involve: (1) specifying the multilevel model with within- and between-group structures, (2) deriving the asymptotic covariance matrix of the DLSM estimator for multilevel parameters, and (3) developing a Lagrange Multiplier (LM) type test statistic to quantify the local sensitivity of each parameter to perturbations in the moment conditions. This test will be integrated into a stepwise model specification search procedure, optimizing computation via score functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide applied researchers with a practical tool for conducting principled, parameter-specific sensitivity analyses within MSEMs, moving beyond global fit. It will enhance the robustness of conclusions drawn from complex hierarchical models by flagging unstable estimates. The method will be implemented in user-friendly software, extending the capabilities of existing SEM packages.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analysis exists for single-level SEM, its formal integration into the estimation and testing workflow for multilevel covariance structures using a distributionally robust least squares approach is novel. The proposed LM-type sensitivity test directly addresses parameter stability within the hierarchical constraint system, a non-trivial extension of prior work.

proposals.title: A Specification Search Framework Using Inverted Goodness-of-Fit Tests for Adequate Covariance Structure Models
proposals.motivation: In covariance structure analysis, the standard use of goodness-of-fit tests (e.g., chi-square) is to reject misspecified models, often leaving researchers with no clear path to find an acceptable model. This binary reject/not-reject framework is problematic in applied research where the goal is to identify a plausible model for interpretation and forecasting, not just to falsify theories. A systematic, principled method to search for adequate, non-rejected models is needed to bridge the gap between theory testing and model development.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the standard likelihood ratio or other discrepancy tests, a computationally efficient search algorithm can systematically identify regions of the model specification space that yield adequate fit according to pre-specified Type I error rates, thereby generating a set of candidate models that are not statistically rejected by the data.
proposals.method: The method will develop an algorithm that treats the test p-value as a function of model parameters and specifications (e.g., fixed/free parameters, cross-loadings, residual covariances). Using a Disturbed Least Squares Method (DLSM) or similar robust estimation core, the algorithm will perform guided specification searches—such as Lagrange Multiplier (LM) test-driven model expansions or Wald test-driven contractions—iteratively adjusting the model until the chi-square test statistic falls below the critical threshold for rejection. The search will be constrained by penalty functions to control parameter proliferation and maintain interpretability, with performance evaluated via Monte Carlo simulation under normal and non-normal error conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, test-based framework for specification searches that moves beyond ad-hoc modification indices, offering a replicable method to find adequate models. It will extend Professor Bentler's work on robust LM tests and fit evaluation by creating a practical tool for exploratory model building within a confirmatory testing paradigm, directly applicable to fields like political science and climate forecasting where model development is data-informed.
proposals.novelty: While modification indices suggest single parameter changes, this framework inverts the entire hypothesis testing procedure to define and navigate a space of 'adequate' models, a goal-oriented approach distinct from pure rejection. It formally integrates robust estimation (DLSM) with an inverted test criterion for search, which is not a standard feature in pre-2026 SEM software.

proposals.title: A Multi-Statistic Fit Assessment Framework for Covariance Structure Analysis: Moving Beyond the Chi-Square Test
proposals.motivation: In covariance structure analysis, model fit is predominantly judged by a single omnibus statistic, such as the chi-square test. This practice is problematic because a model can pass a global test while still failing to capture specific, theoretically important data characteristics, such as the covariance between key variables or the distribution of residuals. A more comprehensive assessment is needed to ensure models are not just statistically adequate but also substantively accurate representations of the underlying data structure.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite fit assessment, requiring a model to simultaneously satisfy multiple, distinct statistical criteria, will identify misspecified models that a single omnibus test would accept, thereby improving the validity of model selection and specification searches.
proposals.method: We propose developing a multi-statistic fit index (MSFI) that integrates pre-2026 established metrics: the normal-theory chi-square, Browne's asymptotically distribution-free (ADF) statistic, and the discrepancy function from the Direct Least Squares Method (DLSM). A model will be deemed to have adequate multi-statistic fit only if it passes bootstrapped goodness-of-fit tests for all constituent statistics at a Bonferroni-adjusted alpha level. The framework will be operationalized within an R package extending the 'lavaan' ecosystem, implementing a sequential testing procedure and providing diagnostic output on which specific criteria a model fails.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, practical tool for applied researchers to conduct more defensible model evaluations, reducing the risk of accepting misspecified models. It will formally bridge robust estimation theory (e.g., ADF) with practical fit assessment, advancing methodological best practices in SEM and related covariance modeling techniques.
proposals.novelty: While the limitations of the chi-square test are well-known, the proposed method's novelty lies in its formal, simultaneous requirement for a model to satisfy multiple, non-redundant statistical criteria, moving beyond ad-hoc fit index tables to a principled, composite hypothesis test.

proposals.title: A Monte Carlo Power Analysis of Alternative Fit Statistics for Detecting Misspecification in Covariance Structure Models
proposals.motivation: In covariance structure analysis, researchers rely on a variety of fit statistics (e.g., chi-square, RMSEA, CFI, SRMR) to evaluate model adequacy. However, pre-2026 literature lacks a systematic, condition-specific understanding of their relative statistical power to detect different types of model misspecification. This gap leads to inconsistent practices and potentially undetected model errors, undermining the validity of substantive conclusions in fields like psychology and political science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the power of fit statistics is not uniform but depends critically on the type of misspecification (e.g., omitted cross-loadings, factor correlations, or residual covariances), sample size, and distributional properties of the data, with robust statistics like those from DLSM (Distributionally-Least-Squares Methods) offering superior power under non-normal conditions.
proposals.method: We will conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation study manipulating factors: sample size (N=100-1000), model type (CFA, SEM), type and magnitude of misspecification, and data distribution (normal, skewed, heavy-tailed). For each condition, we will generate data from a true model, fit a misspecified model, and compute a suite of fit statistics including the standard likelihood ratio test, robust variants (Satorra-Bentler), RMSEA, CFI, SRMR, and statistics derived from DLSM estimation. Power will be calculated as the proportion of replications where each statistic rejects the misspecified model at α=.05. Analysis will use Bentler's EQS software for simulation and estimation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a definitive, empirically-grounded guide for researchers to select the most powerful fit statistic given their research context. It will extend Bentler's work on robust testing by formally integrating DLSM-based statistics into the power comparison framework. The results will lead to more reliable model specification searches and improved methodological practice in applied structural equation modeling.
proposals.novelty: While power studies for individual statistics exist, a comprehensive, multi-condition comparison that includes both traditional and newer robust estimators (like DLSM) for a wide array of misspecification types is novel. The focus on the interaction between estimation method (e.g., ML, DLSM) and the resulting power of associated fit indices moves beyond simple cataloguing to a mechanistic understanding.

proposals.title: Robust Estimation for Ordinal Structural Equation Models via Flexible Link Functions and Full Information Maximum Likelihood
proposals.motivation: Structural equation models with ordinal indicators typically assume a rigid probit or logit link function, which can lead to biased parameter estimates and poor model fit if this assumption is violated. This problem is compounded by missing data, which is common in applied social and behavioral science research. A robust method is needed to ensure valid inference when the standard ordinal link is misspecified.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating flexible, estimable link functions (e.g., via splines or power families) into the estimation framework for ordinal SEM will yield more accurate parameter estimates and better model fit than standard probit/logit models when the link function is misspecified, even in the presence of missing data.
proposals.method: We will extend the Diagonally Weighted Least Squares (DWLS) and Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML) estimators to incorporate a flexible link function for ordinal thresholds. The link function will be parameterized using a monotonic spline or a generalized link family (e.g., scobit). Model estimation will proceed via a two-stage pseudo-maximum likelihood approach: first, estimating the flexible thresholds and polychoric correlations, then fitting the structural model using a robust test statistic (e.g., the Satorra-Bentler scaled chi-square) to correct standard errors. Simulation studies will evaluate performance under various link misspecifications and missingness mechanisms (MAR).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, integrated framework for fitting ordinal SEMs that is robust to link function misspecification. It will extend the toolkit of robust covariance structure analysis by introducing a method analogous to flexible baseline hazard estimation in survival analysis, thereby improving the reliability of conclusions drawn from ordinal data with missing values.
proposals.novelty: While flexible links exist in generalized linear models, their integration into full-information SEM estimation for ordinal data with missingness is novel. The approach directly challenges the fixed link paradigm in ordinal SEM, drawing a principled analogy from advancements in survival analysis.

proposals.title: Personalizing Measurement in Single-Case SEM: A Regularized Least Squares Approach for Link Function Discovery in Ordinal Intensive Longitudinal Data
proposals.motivation: Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) and N-of-1 studies generate intensive ordinal time series to model within-person dynamics. Standard structural equation modeling (SEM) for such data assumes a universal link function (e.g., probit), ignoring individual differences in response style (e.g., extreme vs. middle responding). This misspecification can bias estimates of latent trajectories and dynamic parameters, undermining personalized inference. Pre-2026 methods for handling ordinal data in SEM lack a formal, data-driven framework for personalizing the measurement model at the individual level.
proposals.hypothesis: Treating the ordinal link function as a flexible, person-specific parameter to be estimated from intensive longitudinal data will yield a better-fitting measurement model and more accurate estimates of within-person structural parameters than models imposing a standard link function.
proposals.method: We adapt a regularized least squares (RLS) framework, akin to Bentler's work on distributionally optimal methods, to fit a single-case SEM with an ordinal measurement model. The framework parameterizes a family of candidate link functions (e.g., probit, logit, cauchit, complementary log-log) within a unified representation. A penalized estimation procedure, such as a smoothly clipped absolute deviation (SCAD) penalty on link function deviations from a baseline, will be employed for stable selection and estimation from a single individual's time series. The method will integrate full-information maximum likelihood to handle missing data mechanisms common in EMA.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a novel statistical tool for personalized psychometric modeling within SEM, directly addressing response heterogeneity in intensive longitudinal designs. It extends the principle of model specification search to the N-of-1 context, offering a rigorous test for link function appropriateness. The resulting framework will enhance the validity of inferences about within-person dynamics in clinical, health, and behavioral science research.
proposals.novelty: While link functions are treated as fixed in group-level SEM, this proposal makes them estimable, person-specific parameters. It innovatively applies penalized estimation principles, common in variable selection, to the novel problem of discovering an individual's response mapping within a dynamic model.

proposals.title: Robust Estimation for Ordinal Data with Missingness: A Distributionally Weighted Least Squares Approach with Flexible Link Functions
proposals.motivation: Structural equation modeling (SEM) with ordinal indicators routinely assumes a probit or logit link function, which may be misspecified for real-world data, leading to biased parameter estimates and incorrect inferences. This problem is compounded by missing data, which is ubiquitous in applied social and behavioral science research. A robust, flexible framework is needed to test and relax this critical assumption while properly handling missingness.
proposals.hypothesis: That employing a broader class of link functions (e.g., complementary log-log, cauchit) within a distributionally weighted least squares (DWLS) estimator will yield more accurate parameter estimates and better model fit than standard probit/logit links when the underlying response process is non-standard, even in the presence of missing at random (MAR) data.
proposals.method: We will extend the robust DWLS framework, a standard for ordinal SEM, to incorporate a family of link functions. A two-stage procedure will be developed: first, a model specification search using Lagrange Multiplier (LM) tests to identify the optimal link for each ordinal indicator. Second, a full-information maximum likelihood (FIML) or multiple imputation approach will be integrated to handle missing data during estimation. The performance of the proposed method will be evaluated via Monte Carlo simulations comparing bias, efficiency, and Type I error rates of chi-square tests against standard methods under various link misspecification and missingness scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide applied researchers with a practical, testable method to challenge the default link function assumption, enhancing the validity of conclusions drawn from ordinal SEM. It will contribute new theory and software tools (potentially for R packages like 'lavaan') for robust model specification searches in the presence of incomplete data.
proposals.novelty: While link function selection is known in generalized linear models, its systematic integration and testing within the DWLS-SEM framework for incomplete ordinal data is novel. The combination of flexible links, robust estimation, and principled missing data handling addresses a significant, yet overlooked, gap in covariance structure analysis.

proposals.title: A Link-Agnostic Multiple Imputation Framework for Ordinal Missing Data in Covariance Structure Analysis
proposals.motivation: In structural equation modeling (SEM) with ordinal data, standard multiple imputation methods assume a specific link function (e.g., probit) for the underlying latent variable distribution. This assumption, if incorrect, can introduce bias into subsequent model fit indices and parameter estimates, particularly in complex analyses like measurement invariance testing. The joint problem of link misspecification and missing data remains inadequately addressed, threatening the validity of conclusions in applied research.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning-based imputation framework that learns an appropriate link or latent distribution from the observed ordinal data patterns will produce multiply imputed datasets that yield less biased model fit statistics and parameter estimates in SEM compared to imputation methods assuming a fixed, potentially incorrect, link function.
proposals.method: We will develop a modified multiple imputation by chained equations (MICE) algorithm where the conditional model for each ordinal variable is a non-parametric or flexible semi-parametric learner, such as a random forest or gradient boosting machine, capable of capturing complex relationships without a pre-specified link. The algorithm will be integrated with a latent variable rescaling step to ensure compatibility with the covariance structure model. Validation will involve Monte Carlo simulations manipulating missingness mechanisms, true underlying link functions, and model specifications, with performance evaluated using Dr. Bentler's DLSM-based fit indices and parameter bias.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, robust imputation tool for ordinal missing data that relaxes a critical and often untestable assumption, directly enhancing the reliability of model specification searches and fit evaluation. It will offer new insights into the interplay between measurement model misspecification and missing data handling, a key concern for methodological rigor in fields like political science and climate research where ordinal indicators are common.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in directly challenging the standard probit/logit link assumption within the imputation process itself, using flexible machine learning to *learn* the imputation model rather than imposing it, thereby bridging machine learning robustness with the covariance structure modeling tradition.

proposals.title: A Regularized Bayesian Copula SEM for Ordinal Data with Missingness: Stabilizing Small-Sample Inference
proposals.motivation: Structural equation modeling with ordinal data and missing values is common in psychology and education, but small samples lead to unstable estimates and poor model fit tests. Pre-2026 methods like robust weighted least squares (WLSMV) or full-information maximum likelihood require large samples, while existing Bayesian SEMs often assume normality for latent responses. There is a critical need for a method that simultaneously handles non-normal ordinal data, missingness, and small-sample instability within a unified, principled framework.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating Bayesian regularization with a Gaussian copula model for the latent responses will produce more stable and accurate parameter estimates and superior model fit recovery for ordinal SEM with missing data in small samples, compared to existing frequentist or unregularized Bayesian approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel objective function fusing a penalized likelihood, akin to a Regularized Least Squares (RLS) estimator, with a Gaussian copula model for the multivariate dependence of the underlying latent variables. Informative, shrinkage-inducing priors (e.g., horseshoe or lasso-type) will be placed on copula correlation and structural model parameters. Estimation will proceed via a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm, implementing a data augmentation step for the latent responses and missing data, and a Gibbs/Metropolis-Hastings sampling scheme for the regularized parameters. Model fit will be evaluated using posterior predictive p-values and information criteria (DIC, WAIC).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a statistically rigorous, all-in-one toolbox for applied researchers facing small-sample ordinal data with missing values, improving inference reliability. It will extend Bentler's work on model fit evaluation and least-squares estimation into the Bayesian regularization domain. The methodology will be implemented in user-friendly R/Stan code to facilitate adoption.
proposals.novelty: The direct fusion of a penalized RLS-style objective with a copula model for latent variable dependence is novel, as is the use of informative priors specifically to regularize the copula parameters themselves, which stabilizes the core dependence structure in small samples where it is most fragile.

proposals.title: A Positive Fit Index for Covariance Structure Models: Development and Validation of a Direct Least Squares Goodness-of-Fit Criterion
proposals.motivation: Traditional model evaluation in Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) is dominated by the logic of null hypothesis significance testing, where the primary goal is to reject a model as a poor fit. This leads to a negative, rejection-oriented paradigm that is often uninformative for model selection among plausible, approximately correct specifications. A shift towards positive identification of well-fitting models is needed to support substantive theory development.
proposals.hypothesis: A goodness-of-fit index can be constructed from the direct least squares (DLS) discrepancy function that provides a positive, continuous measure of model adequacy, where values below a defined threshold provide strong, affirmative evidence for model acceptance, analogous to a confidence interval for fit.
proposals.method: We will derive a new index, the DLS Goodness-of-Fit Criterion (DLS-GFC), based on the minimized value of the DLS discrepancy function. Its sampling distribution will be investigated via extensive Monte Carlo simulations under correct and approximately correct models, varying sample size and distributional conditions (normal and non-normal). We will establish empirical acceptance thresholds (e.g., 90th percentile of the distribution under correct models) and compare its performance in model selection to traditional chi-square tests and approximate fit indices (CFI, RMSEA) using known population models. The method will be integrated into existing SEM software for practical application.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide researchers with a statistically principled tool to affirmatively identify well-fitting models, moving beyond mere failure to reject. It will offer a complementary perspective to chi-square tests, enhancing the toolkit for model fit evaluation in SEM and related covariance structure analyses as advocated in Bentler's recent work on optimizing fit evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While fit indices like CFI and RMSEA evaluate approximate fit, they remain tethered to the rejection framework of the chi-square test. The proposed DLS-GFC inverts this goal by defining a positive region of acceptance based on the distribution of the fit function itself, providing a direct, non-centrality-based affirmation of model adequacy.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Robustness of SEM Fit Index Cutoffs Under Complex Model Misspecification and High Dimensionality
proposals.motivation: In covariance structure analysis, researchers heavily rely on fit index cutoffs (e.g., CFI > 0.95, RMSEA < 0.06) for model evaluation. However, the empirical performance of these widely adopted thresholds has been primarily validated on relatively simple models with limited numbers of variables and basic misspecifications. It is unknown whether these cutoffs remain valid for the increasingly complex, high-dimensional models used in modern social and behavioral science research, potentially leading to erroneous model acceptance or rejection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the established cutoff criteria for common fit indices (e.g., CFI, TLI, RMSEA, SRMR) will demonstrate significant instability and reduced diagnostic accuracy when applied to models with complex, non-local misspecifications and models with a large number of observed variables.
proposals.method: We will conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation study. Data will be generated from a population model and fit to a misspecified target model. We will systematically vary: (1) model complexity (number of latent factors, cross-loadings, residual covariances), (2) type and magnitude of misspecification (omitted nonlinearities, factor correlations, or specific paths), and (3) the number of observed variables (from 10 to 50+). For each condition, we will compute standard fit indices and their known population values. We will then use Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve analysis and distributional methods to evaluate the sensitivity and specificity of existing cutoffs for detecting misspecification, employing robust estimation methods like Diagonally Weighted Least Squares (DWLS) for non-normal data where appropriate.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first comprehensive evaluation of fit index cutoff robustness under conditions of high model complexity. It will yield practical, evidence-based guidelines for researchers using complex SEMs, helping to prevent model mis-specification. The work will also contribute methodologically by illustrating how simulation design must evolve to match contemporary modeling practices.
proposals.novelty: While cutoff evaluation is not new, prior studies have not systematically investigated the joint impact of complex, non-local misspecifications and high dimensionality on cutoff performance, a critical gap given the trend towards larger, more intricate models in applied research.

proposals.title: Influence Diagnostics for Covariance Structure Models: Extending Regression Concepts to SEM
proposals.motivation: Covariance structure models, including SEM, are widely used in social and behavioral sciences, but tools for diagnosing influential observations remain underdeveloped compared to regression. Current model fit evaluations (e.g., chi-square tests) are sensitive to outliers and influential data points, which can lead to misleading conclusions about model specification and parameter estimates. Robust model specification searches, a key theme in Bentler's recent work, require methods to systematically identify data points that exert undue influence on the fitted covariance matrix and overall model fit.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that analogs of regression diagnostics—specifically leverage (based on the model-implied covariance matrix) and influence (combining leverage and residual covariance)—can be formally defined and computed for covariance structure models, and that these measures will effectively identify observations that distort parameter estimates and global fit statistics.
proposals.method: We will derive influence measures by extending the Discrepancy-based Least Squares Method (DLSM) framework. Leverage will be conceptualized using the Hessian of the fitting function. Cook's distance and DFBETAS analogs will be developed by examining the change in the fitted covariance matrix and key parameter estimates upon deletion of each case. Computational formulas will be derived for normal-theory maximum likelihood and robust estimators. The performance of these diagnostics will be evaluated via Monte Carlo simulation under various contamination models and compared to existing case-deletion approaches.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide applied researchers with a practical set of diagnostic tools, integrated into standard SEM software, to screen for influential observations before final model interpretation. It will enhance the robustness of model specification searches by making the sensitivity of chi-square tests and parameter estimates to individual data points transparent, directly supporting Bentler's focus on improving model fit evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While case deletion is known, a systematic translation and formalization of regression-style *incremental* influence and leverage diagnostics for the covariance matrix itself, rather than just mean structures, is novel. This approach moves beyond overall fit indices to offer targeted, observation-level diagnostics specific to covariance structure analysis.

proposals.title: Adjusted Cutoff Criteria for Fit Indices in Small-Sample Structural Equation Modeling: A Distributionally Limited Sample Method (DLSM) Approach
proposals.motivation: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) relies heavily on fit indices (e.g., CFI, RMSEA, SRMR) with established cutoff criteria (e.g., Hu & Bentler, 1999). However, these cutoffs were derived from simulation studies with moderate to large sample sizes (N > 250). In fields like political science or specialized clinical research, sample sizes are often very small (N < 50), where standard cutoffs are known to be biased and overly restrictive, leading to excessive model rejection. Pre-2026 literature lacks principled, adjusted criteria for such small-N contexts, hindering valid model evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically modeling the distributional behavior of common fit indices under very small sample conditions using robust estimation methods, we can derive and validate adjusted cutoff criteria that maintain nominal Type I error rates and provide accurate model fit assessment.
proposals.method: We will employ a Monte Carlo simulation framework, generating data from known population models with very small sample sizes (N = 20 to 100). Building on Bentler's recent work on the Distributionally Limited Sample Method (DLSM), we will analyze the empirical sampling distributions of fit indices (CFI, RMSEA, SRMR) under both normal and non-normal conditions. The core technical approach involves using DLSM-based estimates to model the relationship between sample size, distribution, and fit index values, then employing quantile regression to derive adjusted cutoffs that correspond to traditional Type I error rates (e.g., α = .05). Validation will involve applying new cutoffs to independent simulated and real-world small-sample datasets.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first set of empirically grounded, adjusted cutoff criteria for major SEM fit indices specifically validated for very small sample sizes. It will directly enhance the practical utility of SEM in data-scarce research domains by offering a defensible standard for model evaluation. The work will extend the application of DLSM from parameter estimation to the critical area of model fit assessment.
proposals.novelty: While small-sample issues in SEM are recognized, prior work focuses on parameter estimation bias or power; a systematic derivation of adjusted global fit index cutoffs using a robust method like DLSM is novel. This moves beyond simply noting the problem to providing a concrete, methodologically sound solution.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Robustness of Fit Indices and Establishing Empirical Cutoffs for High-Dimensional Covariance Structure Models
proposals.motivation: The application of structural equation modeling (SEM) is expanding into fields like genomics, neuroimaging, and large-scale social surveys, where models with hundreds of observed variables are increasingly common. Pre-2026 research, including Bentler's own work on chi-square tests and robust specification searches, has established that traditional fit index behavior and heuristic cutoffs (e.g., for CFI, RMSEA, SRMR) are derived from simulations with much smaller models. Their validity and performance in high-dimensional settings remain largely unknown, risking severe model misspecification and erroneous scientific conclusions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sampling distributions and optimal cutoff values for common approximate fit indices (e.g., CFI, RMSEA, SRMR) systematically shift as the number of observed variables (p) increases relative to sample size (N), and that new, dimensionally-aware cutoffs are required for valid model evaluation.
proposals.method: We will conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation study manipulating model dimensionality (p from 50 to 500), sample size (N), degree of misspecification, and estimation method (Maximum Likelihood, Robust variants, and Distributionally-weighted Least Squares - DLSM). Data will be generated from known population models with controlled misspecifications. We will analyze the empirical distributions of fit indices under correct and misspecified models to derive empirical Type I error and power curves. The core analytical framework will extend Bentler's work on LM tests and robust chi-square evaluation to high-dimensional contexts.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first comprehensive set of empirically-grounded guidelines and potential adjusted cutoff values for fit indices in high-dimensional SEM. It will directly inform practitioners in data-rich disciplines on how to reliably evaluate model fit, enhancing the rigor of applications in political science, climate modeling, and beyond. The work will also contribute methodologically by characterizing the asymptotic behavior of fit indices under high-dimensional regimes.
proposals.novelty: While prior research has examined fit index performance, no systematic pre-2026 study has focused on the scale of hundreds of variables, nor proposed cutoffs that explicitly account for dimensionality. This bridges Bentler's expertise in covariance structure testing with the emerging 'big data' challenges in SEM.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Implicit Association Test (IAT) Structure: A Comparative Fit Analysis of One-Factor, Multidimensional, and Second-Order Models
proposals.motivation: The Implicit Association Test (IAT) is a cornerstone tool in social psychology, widely assumed to measure implicit attitudes via a second-order three-factor structure (Greenwald et al., 2003). However, recent pre-2026 methodological critiques suggest this complex structure may not be universally optimal, potentially leading to misinterpretation of construct validity and biased parameter estimates in applied research. A rigorous, data-driven test of competing structural models is needed to ensure the IAT's theoretical foundations are empirically sound.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for many IAT datasets, a simpler one-factor model or an alternative multidimensional (correlated first-order factors) model will provide a statistically superior and more parsimonious fit to the data than the canonical second-order three-factor structure.
proposals.method: We will employ covariance structure analysis, specifically Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), to test competing models. Using multiple large-scale IAT datasets, we will fit the standard second-order model, a one-factor model, and alternative multidimensional models. Model estimation will utilize robust methods, including Diagonally Weighted Least Squares (DWLS) for ordinal indicators and Maximum Likelihood with Satorra-Bentler corrections for non-normality. Model comparison will rely on the robust chi-square difference test (Satorra & Bentler, 2001), alongside practical fit indices (RMSEA, CFI, SRMR) and information criteria (AIC, BIC).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a definitive, methodologically rigorous evaluation of the IAT's factorial structure, clarifying the instrument's psychometric properties. It will offer concrete guidance to researchers on optimal model specification for IAT data analysis, thereby improving the accuracy of substantive conclusions drawn from this measure in fields like political science and climate change attitude research.
proposals.novelty: While the second-order model is the accepted standard, this study systematically challenges its universality using Bentler's robust testing framework and modern fit evaluation practices, moving beyond simple replication to a principled, competitive model test that is not commonly applied to IAT validation.

proposals.title: Testing Measurement Invariance of Latent Means in Cross-National SEM: A Robust Multi-Group Approach Using Diverse and High-Risk Samples
proposals.motivation: In cross-national structural equation modeling (SEM), the common practice of assuming equal latent means across groups for model identification is often a convenience rather than a tested hypothesis. This assumption, if violated, can bias estimates of structural relationships and lead to erroneous substantive conclusions about country-level differences. Pre-2026 research, including Bentler's work on robust specification searches, highlights the need for rigorous testing of all model constraints, yet latent mean invariance is frequently overlooked in applied political and social science research.
proposals.hypothesis: Actively testing for latent mean differences across countries using diverse or high-risk samples will reveal significant violations of the equal-means assumption that are systematically related to sample characteristics and cultural dimensions, thereby challenging the default practice of constrained estimation.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-group SEM framework, extending Bentler's work on robust Lagrange Multiplier (LM) tests and distributionally robust methods. The core technical approach involves a sequential testing procedure: 1) establishing configural and metric invariance, 2) applying a series of robust LM tests to systematically free latent mean constraints, and 3) utilizing Distributionally-Loaded Structural Modeling (DLSM) or similar robust estimators to handle non-normal data common in high-risk samples. The analysis will be applied to existing cross-national datasets (e.g., World Values Survey, PISA) supplemented with purposefully sampled data from politically unstable or culturally distinct regions.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a formal, robust methodology for empirically testing latent mean invariance, moving it from an identification assumption to a testable hypothesis. It will yield practical guidelines for when and how to relax mean constraints, enhancing the validity of cross-national comparisons. The work directly extends Bentler's contributions to model fit evaluation and robust specification searches into the critical domain of mean structures.
proposals.novelty: While multi-group SEM often tests factor loadings and intercepts, the explicit, data-driven inversion of the equal latent means assumption as a starting point for model specification is a non-standard, hypothesis-driven approach. Combining this with robust tests and deliberately diverse samples moves beyond typical convenience samples to stress-test the assumption's generalizability.

proposals.title: Revisiting Penalization in Fit Indices: A Scaling-Informed Framework for Complexity-Aware Model Selection in Covariance Structure Analysis
proposals.motivation: Model selection in Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) relies heavily on fit indices like CFI, TLI, and RMSEA, which incorporate penalties for model complexity to balance fit and parsimony. However, the scaling of these penalties is often ad-hoc or derived under restrictive assumptions (e.g., multivariate normality), potentially leading to biased selections with modern, complex models or non-normal data. A principled re-examination of how scaling mechanisms penalize complexity is needed to improve the reliability of model selection.
proposals.hypothesis: By systematically inverting and re-engineering the scaling factors used in common fit indices, we can derive a new class of indices whose penalty functions are more sensitive to true model complexity and robust to distributional violations, leading to more accurate model selection.
proposals.method: We will employ a theoretical and simulation-based approach. First, we will deconstruct the scaling mechanisms in indices like TLI and CFI, formally isolating their complexity penalty terms. We will then propose inverted or re-scaled variants, potentially leveraging robust scaling factors from Bentler's earlier work on DLS (Distributionally-Lagged Satorra-Bentler) and robust tests. The performance of new indices will be evaluated via extensive Monte Carlo simulations, varying model complexity, sample size, and distributional conditions (normal, skewed, heavy-tailed), using known population models as a gold standard for selection accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, theoretically-grounded framework for tuning penalization in fit indices, moving beyond fixed formulas. It will deliver practical, improved fit indices for researchers, directly enhancing the methodology central to Bentler's work on covariance structure analysis and model testing. The outcomes will offer 'practical tips' akin to Bentler's recent papers but focused on the foundational mechanics of selection indices.
proposals.novelty: While existing work optimizes test statistics or uses data-driven pathways, the core innovation here is the targeted inversion and recalibration of the scaling penalty itself—a fundamental but under-examined component. This approach is non-obvious as it challenges the conventional construction of fit indices rather than merely applying them.

proposals.title: Software Implementation and Validation of a New Scaled Difference Test for Nested Model Comparison in Structural Equation Modeling
proposals.motivation: In structural equation modeling (SEM), the chi-square difference test is the standard method for comparing nested models, but it is highly sensitive to violations of distributional assumptions, particularly non-normality. This limits its reliability in applied research where data often deviate from multivariate normality. A robust, easy-to-compute scaled difference test, building on Satorra and Bentler's (2001) principles, is needed to provide valid inferences under common non-ideal conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a newly proposed computation method for a scaled chi-square difference test, which corrects for non-normality using robust scaling factors, can be successfully integrated into major SEM software packages and will demonstrate superior Type I error control and power compared to the standard difference test when applied to real-world, non-normal data.
proposals.method: The project will first formalize the algorithm for the new scaled difference test, extending the principles of the Satorra-Bentler scaled test statistic and the robust mean-and-variance adjusted test (e.g., MLM, MLMV). The core computation will involve calculating scaling correction factors for the nested models and deriving the appropriate adjusted difference test. This algorithm will then be implemented as a new function or module in open-source R packages (e.g., lavaan) and commercial software (e.g., Mplus) via user-defined functions. The implementation will be validated through extensive Monte Carlo simulations, varying sample size, model complexity, and degree of non-normality, to assess its statistical properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide applied researchers with a readily accessible, robust tool for nested model comparison, directly enhancing the rigor of model selection in fields like psychology, education, and political science. It will bridge the gap between advanced methodological development and everyday practice by embedding a theoretically sound correction into standard software workflows.
proposals.novelty: While scaled test statistics exist, the novelty lies in the development and dissemination of a specifically optimized and computationally straightforward *difference* test for nested models, and its direct integration into multiple software platforms, moving it from a theoretical formula to a practical, default-ready option for analysts.

proposals.title: A Monte Carlo Power Analysis of Scaled vs. Unscaled Chi-Square Difference Tests in Structural Equation Modeling
proposals.motivation: In covariance structure analysis, the chi-square difference test is a cornerstone for nested model comparison, but its validity relies on correct model specification and distributional assumptions. Scaled test statistics (e.g., Satorra-Bentler) were developed to correct bias under non-normality, yet their comparative statistical power for detecting true model differences, especially against unscaled tests, remains insufficiently characterized. A systematic power analysis is needed to guide applied researchers in choosing the most sensitive test under realistic data conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the new scaled difference test will maintain superior Type I error control under non-normality while exhibiting comparable or greater statistical power than the traditional unscaled difference test, particularly in small-to-moderate samples and for models with complex error structures.
proposals.method: We will conduct an extensive Monte Carlo simulation study manipulating factors: sample size (N=100 to 1000), degree of non-normality (varying skewness/kurtosis), model complexity, and size of the true nested parameter difference. Data will be generated from a known population model, and both the scaled (Satorra-Bentler type) and unscaled chi-square difference tests will be applied to nested model comparisons. Power will be calculated as the proportion of replications correctly rejecting the false, more restrictive model. Analysis will utilize the 'lavaan' package in R, implementing the DLSM (Distributionally-Loaded Structural Modeling) estimator for robust parameter estimates as a foundation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a definitive, empirical power curve for scaled difference tests, offering practical guidelines for model selection in applied SEM. It will extend Bentler's work on robust test statistics by quantifying the trade-off between robustness and power, directly informing best practices in fields like political science and environmental forecasting where model specification searches are common.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on Type I error correction of scaled tests, a comprehensive, factorially designed power comparison against unscaled benchmarks, specifically for the *difference* test in the context of modern robust estimators, represents a novel and critical advancement for methodological practice.

ideas.title: Bayesian Covariance-Focused FIML for Small Samples with Ordinal Missing Data
ideas.description: This research develops a small-sample, Bayesian-approximated Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML) variant specifically designed for scenarios where only the covariance structure is of substantive interest, such as in factor analysis or network models. It integrates the core insight of Idea A—decoupling estimation from mean structure modeling—with Idea B's use of Bayesian approximations (e.g., variational Bayes or integrated nested Laplace approximations) to stabilize estimation with ordinal missing data in tiny samples (n < 50). The resulting method provides principled, regularized estimates of covariance parameters and latent variable relationships without the risk of contamination from a misspecified mean model, a critical advancement for psychological and social science research with limited ordinal data.

ideas.title: High-Dimensional Ordinal SEM with Missing Data via Regularized FIML
ideas.description: This research fuses scalable Regularized Least Squares (RLS) for SEM with extensions for high-dimensional ordinal missing data. The core idea is to develop a regularized Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML) estimator that incorporates sparsity-inducing penalties (like lasso or ridge) directly into the likelihood for ordinal data. This integrated approach allows for stable parameter estimation and model selection in structural equation models where the number of latent constructs and observed ordinal indicators vastly exceeds the sample size, and where missing data is pervasive. The resulting methodology would enable the analysis of complex psychological or social science theories using real-world large-scale survey data that is both high-dimensional and incomplete.

ideas.title: A Theory-Driven Mapping of Small-Sample Correction Performance for Maximum Likelihood
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a simple benchmark to develop a predictive framework for small-sample corrections to ML fit statistics. It uses a comprehensive simulation design that systematically manipulates theoretically relevant conditions (sample size, number of indicators, distributional violations, model misspecification) to test Regularized Likelihood Scaling (RLS), scaled chi-square, and bootstrap methods. Performance is evaluated on multiple criteria including bias, Type I error rate, and power to detect misspecification. The outcome is a set of empirically grounded, conditional guidelines that allow researchers to select the most robust correction method for their specific analytical context.

ideas.title: Robustness-Weighted SEM for Small Samples with Incomplete Data
ideas.description: This research develops a sensitivity analysis framework for small-sample Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with incomplete data, moving beyond a single assumption of Missing at Random (MAR). The core method adapts the Regularized Least Squares (RLS) estimator to function within a pre-specified class of Missing Not at Random (MNAR) mechanisms, quantifying how parameter estimates and inferences change as the assumption deviates from MAR. The output is not a single point estimate but a robustness profile or weighted ensemble of estimates, explicitly communicating the dependence of conclusions on untestable assumptions. This approach provides a principled, transparent way to assess the stability of findings in small-sample studies where the MAR assumption is particularly precarious.

ideas.title: Robust M-estimation for invariance testing under joint misspecification of measurement model and data mechanism
ideas.description: This research develops a unified robust estimation procedure for measurement invariance testing with ordinal data that is resistant to both anomalous observations (outliers) and fundamental misspecification of the measurement model's factor structure. It investigates how robust M-estimators, which use influence functions to downweight outliers, perform when the assumed threshold structure or factor loadings are incorrect and data are missing not at random (MNAR). The core synergy is a diagnostic framework that can distinguish whether detected 'invariance violations' are driven by true population differences, localized outliers, model misspecification, or their complex interplay, leading to more reliable substantive conclusions.

ideas.title: Robust Regularized RLS for Complex SEM with Categorical and Interaction Terms
ideas.description: This research investigates the performance of Regularized Least Squares (RLS) estimation in complex Structural Equation Models (SEM) featuring categorical indicators and latent interaction terms. The core innovation is to systematically relax the assumption of an unbiased, well-conditioned covariance matrix by employing regularization techniques (e.g., ridge, lasso, or eigenvalue shrinkage) specifically tailored to the covariance structures generated by these complex models. This approach directly tackles the extreme ill-conditioning and high-dimensionality introduced by dummy coding categorical variables and forming product terms for interactions, which render standard RLS inapplicable. The result is a robust, generalized RLS framework capable of providing stable parameter estimates and model fit assessments for advanced SEM specifications previously outside the scope of distributionally-robust estimation methods.

ideas.title: A Decision Framework for Estimator Selection: Benchmarking RLS Against Robust Alternatives in Complex SEM
ideas.description: This research moves beyond descriptive comparison to develop a practical decision framework for selecting estimators in complex SEM with categorical variables or interactions. It rigorously benchmarks Robust Least Squares (RLS) against established robust alternatives (e.g., MLR, WLSMV, bootstrap methods) using a multi-criteria evaluation that includes parameter bias, Type I/II error rates, computational speed, and ease of implementation under realistic data challenges. The outcome is a set of empirically grounded guidelines that help researchers choose the most appropriate estimator based on their specific data characteristics and study goals, directly addressing the 'so what?' limitation of pure performance examination.

ideas.title: Bayesian Regularized Copula SEM for Ordinal Missing Data with Small Samples
ideas.description: This research integrates Bayesian regularization directly into a copula-based structural equation modeling (SEM) framework to handle non-normal ordinal data with missingness, particularly in small-sample contexts. It develops a novel objective function that combines the penalized likelihood of a Regularized Least Squares (RLS) estimator with a copula model for the multivariate dependence of the latent responses and informative priors for the copula parameters. This synergy allows for flexible modeling of complex, non-normal dependence structures while using prior knowledge to stabilize the estimation of copula and structural parameters where data is sparse or missing. The resulting methodology provides a principled way to obtain reliable inferences in small-sample SEM applications, such as in psychological or educational measurement, where ordinal items with missing data are common.

ideas.title: N-of-1 Structural Equation Modeling with MNAR Sensitivity Analysis for Intensive Longitudinal Ordinal Data
ideas.description: This research develops and evaluates methods for fitting single-case (N-of-1) structural equation models (SEM) to intensive longitudinal ordinal data, such as daily symptom ratings. It specifically extends the evaluation of estimation methods, including adapted regularized least squares (RLS), to scenarios where data are Missing Not at Random (MNAR) in ways plausible for an individual (e.g., mood-dependent skipping of surveys). The synergy lies in creating a principled, person-specific sensitivity analysis toolbox that allows researchers to assess how robust an individual's inferred dynamic network or causal structure is to different assumptions about their unique missing data mechanism, moving beyond population-averaged MNAR models.

ideas.title: A Unified Framework for Small-Sample Inference: Robust RLS Standard Errors and Comparative Calibration
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the development of robust standard errors for Regularized Likelihood Shrinkage (RLS) with a comprehensive benchmarking study against other small-sample corrections (e.g., scaled chi-square, bootstrap). The core synergy is to use the newly derived RLS standard error formulas as a precise diagnostic tool within the comparative study, allowing for a decomposition of error into bias and variance components for each method. This enables the creation of a principled, condition-specific guideline or even an adaptive meta-procedure that selects or optimally weights inferences from RLS, ML with corrections, or bootstrap based on which provides the most accurate coverage for the given sample size, model complexity, and data characteristics.

ideas.title: Link-Agnostic Machine Learning Imputation for Ordinal Data in Structural Models
ideas.description: This research develops a novel machine learning imputation framework for ordinal missing data in structural equation modeling that does not assume a probit or logit link function a priori. The method adapts flexible imputation algorithms (e.g., random forests, chained equations) to learn an appropriate link function or latent distribution from the observed data patterns during the imputation process itself. This allows for the generation of multiply imputed datasets that are consistent with a more general class of latent variable models, directly challenging the standard link function assumption. The approach is then validated within measurement invariance testing, where the consequences of link function misspecification and missing data are jointly examined for bias in model fit and parameter estimates.

ideas.title: A Decision Framework for Model Diagnostics: Comparing the Relative Diagnostic Power of RLS and Alternatives in Small Samples
ideas.description: This study moves beyond quantifying the absolute power of the Ramsey Regression Equation Specification Error Test (RLS) to developing a practical decision framework for applied researchers. It conducts a comprehensive simulation comparing the diagnostic performance of RLS against a curated set of alternative misspecification indicators—including graphical methods, influence metrics, and competing tests—under common small-sample conditions and varied misspecification types (e.g., omitted variables, incorrect functional form, heteroskedasticity). The analysis evaluates not only statistical power but also false alarm rates and the robustness of each indicator to small-n idiosyncrasies. The result is an evidence-based guideline that recommends the most informative diagnostic procedure or combination of procedures for given research constraints, transforming a known limitation into actionable methodological advice.

ideas.title: Personalized Link Function Discovery for N-of-1 Ordinal Time Series
ideas.description: This research develops a novel framework for single-case structural equation modeling (SEM) that treats the link function in ordinal measurement models as a flexible, person-specific parameter to be estimated. By adapting regularized least squares (RLS) principles or similar penalized estimation techniques, the method will fit intensive longitudinal data from one individual, testing a family of alternative link functions (e.g., cauchit, complementary log-log) against the standard probit/logit. The approach explicitly handles missing data mechanisms common in ecological momentary assessment (EMA) and diary studies. The core innovation is the ability to statistically identify which link function best characterizes a specific individual's response style, thereby personalizing the measurement model for more accurate inference about within-person dynamics.

ideas.title: Adapting Robust Link Functions from Survival Analysis for Ordinal Data with Missingness
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from survival analysis, where challenging the standard proportional hazards link function is common practice to handle model misspecification and complex censoring. The analogous solution involves employing flexible, alternative link functions or non-parametric estimation for the baseline hazard. Adapted to the context of ordinal data in structural models, the research idea is to develop and test methods that treat the link function for ordinal observed variables as a flexible component to be estimated or robustly selected. This would be integrated into a structural equation modeling framework capable of handling missing data (e.g., using FIML), thereby creating a more robust alternative to models that rigidly assume a probit or logit link. The goal is to improve inference and model fit when the standard ordinal link function assumption is violated.

ideas.title: Fast Approximate Regularized Least Squares for High-Power Invariance Testing with Ordinal Data
ideas.description: This research develops a computationally efficient, approximate Regularized Least Squares (RLS) algorithm specifically optimized for detecting measurement noninvariance (e.g., Differential Item Functioning) in large-sample ordinal data applications, such as international educational assessments or massive online surveys. The algorithm's regularization and approximation schemes are explicitly tuned to prioritize statistical power (minimize Type II errors) in identifying potential noninvariance, rather than strictly controlling Type I error. This allows researchers to rapidly screen thousands of items and group comparisons for potential bias, flagging candidates for subsequent rigorous, exact analysis. Thus, the method synergistically achieves scalable computation for large n while directly addressing the core inferential risk of missing meaningful invariance violations.

ideas.title: Robust N-of-1 Dynamic Modeling with Systematic Missingness
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the principles of the Regularized Least Squares (RLS) estimator to develop a novel methodology for single-case (N-of-1) dynamic SEM. The core innovation is adapting RLS to handle incomplete intensive longitudinal data from one unit under Missing at Random (MAR) mechanisms, which are prevalent in ecological momentary assessment studies due to participant non-response or device failure. The fused approach explicitly models the temporal dependencies within the individual while applying regularization to stabilize parameter estimates and directly account for the pattern of missingness in the likelihood or estimation procedure. This enables the investigation of personalized psychological or physiological processes with greater validity and statistical rigor, despite the imperfect data collection inherent to real-world single-subject research.

ideas.title: Robust Regularized Estimation for Measurement Invariance with Complete Noninvariance and Ill-Conditioned Data
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework that addresses the joint problem of complete noninvariance (where some groups lack data for entire item blocks) and severe ill-conditioning of within-group covariance matrices. It integrates regularized covariance estimation (e.g., shrinkage, banding) directly into the estimation process for measurement invariance models, treating the missing data patterns induced by noninvariance as a structured missingness problem. The synergy allows for stable parameter recovery and hypothesis testing about latent means and variances by borrowing strength across groups through regularization, even when the per-group observed data matrix is singular or near-singular due to the combination of limited sample size and complete noninvariance. This creates a novel capability for robust cross-group comparison in high-dimensional, fragmented datasets common in integrative data analysis or multinational assessments.

ideas.title: Apply Bayesian Phylogenetic Comparative Methods to Inform Missing Data Priors in Invariance Testing
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from evolutionary biology, where Bayesian phylogenetic comparative methods use the tree structure as a prior to handle missing trait data. The analogous problem in psychometrics is ordinal missing data in multi-group invariance testing. The adaptation treats the latent variable model's implied covariance structure across groups as an informative prior framework for the missing data mechanism. By embedding this structural prior within a Bayesian estimation, the method can more robustly handle missing ordinal data that is not missing at random, improving the accuracy of invariance tests by probabilistically informing the missing values based on the model and the observed data patterns.

ideas.title: Doubly Robust Covariance Estimation via Joint Mean-Covariance Regularization
ideas.description: This method develops a regularized least squares framework that jointly estimates mean and covariance structures with a focus on robust covariance recovery. It employs a doubly robust estimation strategy where the covariance estimate remains consistent under misspecification of either the mean model or a preliminary working covariance model. The approach uses penalization to control the complexity of both components, preventing overfitting and ensuring stable estimation even when the primary interest is in the covariance. This guarantees that covariance estimates are reliable regardless of whether the mean structure is perfectly known, simple, or complex.

ideas.title: Bayesian Bootstrap Inference for Small-Sample RLS with Ordinal Missing Data
ideas.description: This research synthesizes bootstrap resampling and small-sample Bayesian approximations to develop a novel inference method for Regularized Least Squares (RLS) models with ordinal missing data. It employs a Bayesian bootstrap procedure, where resampling weights are drawn from a Dirichlet distribution, to generate posterior distributions for RLS parameters. This approach integrates seamlessly with small-sample variants of pFIML/lFIML, using the bootstrap replicates to approximate the posterior of model parameters and hyperparameters (like regularization strength) without relying on large-sample assumptions. The result is a computationally tractable, fully Bayesian nonparametric framework that delivers valid uncertainty quantification for regularized estimates in small samples with incomplete ordinal data.

ideas.title: Robustness Benchmark of Missing Data Methods for Invariance Testing Under Adversarial Practical Conditions
ideas.description: This study conducts a comprehensive simulation benchmark comparing FIML and modern multiple imputation for ordinal missing data in measurement invariance testing. The key innovation is evaluating methods not under ideal assumptions alone, but within an adversarial simulation space that combines realistic threats like MNAR missingness mechanisms, minor model misspecifications, and small-to-moderate sample sizes. Performance is assessed primarily on the practical metrics of Type I error inflation and power for detecting non-invariance under these complex conditions. The results will provide applied researchers with a robust, survivability-focused guide for choosing a missing data method when conducting invariance tests with real-world, imperfect data.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Robust Regression Estimators Against RLS Under Systematic Violations of Multivariate Normality
ideas.description: This research extends the Monte Carlo simulation into a comprehensive benchmarking study. It systematically generates data violating multivariate normality (e.g., through t-distributions, skew-normal distributions, and mixture models for contamination) and tests not only RLS but a curated set of robust regression estimators. The analysis compares estimators on multiple performance criteria under each violation scenario. The final output is an evidence-based guideline that maps specific types of distributional violations to the most reliable regression methodology, moving beyond mere description to actionable recommendation.

ideas.title: Adapting Single-Subject Time-Series Analysis from Behavioral Pharmacology to N-of-1 SEM
ideas.description: This idea explores adapting Recursive Least Squares (RLS) principles for intensive longitudinal data from a single unit (N-of-1) by drawing an analogy from behavioral pharmacology's single-subject experimental designs. In that field, causal inferences are made for an individual by comparing observed time-series patterns against a null distribution generated from the individual's own data under different experimental phases (e.g., baseline vs. treatment), using methods like simulation modeling analysis. The adapted solution applies this logic to N-of-1 Structural Equation Modeling (SEM). The research would develop a framework where RLS-SEM parameters (e.g., dynamic path coefficients) estimated from an individual's intensive longitudinal data are evaluated for significance and stability using a within-person resampling or permutation method. This could involve bootstrapping contiguous time blocks, permuting the temporal order of variables within constraints, or simulating data based on the individual's own estimated error structures to create a person-specific reference distribution for hypothesis testing, thereby enabling formal inference for single-case SEM without relying on cross-sectional or multi-subject assumptions.

ideas.title: Identifiable Cross-Group Latent Bridging with Anchor Items and Predictive Validation
ideas.description: This research develops a robust framework for handling data missing completely due to noninvariant items by integrating a minimal set of invariant anchor items administered across all groups to ensure identifiability. The core method uses these anchors to statistically bridge the latent scales, then employs multiple imputation and model-based procedures to handle the completely missing items, explicitly quantifying the uncertainty introduced. The approach is validated through a tiered strategy using sensitivity analysis to different bridging assumptions and by testing the predictive relationship of the recovered latent scores on external, real-world outcomes not used in model fitting.

ideas.title: Adapting Bootstrap Methods from Population Ecology to Derive Small-Sample Standard Errors for RLS
ideas.description: The core problem of estimating uncertainty for model parameters with limited, non-independent data has a strong parallel in population ecology, where scientists estimate survival rates from small mark-recapture datasets. The analogous solution is the use of non-parametric bootstrap resampling to build empirical confidence intervals. This research idea adapts that solution to Recursive Least Squares (RLS) estimation. We propose to develop and validate a bootstrap methodology specifically for RLS parameters in small-sample settings. The procedure would involve resampling the sequential data or innovations to generate a distribution of parameter estimates, from which robust standard errors and small-sample confidence intervals can be derived and validated through simulation studies against traditional asymptotic formulas.

ideas.title: Bayesian Robustness Evaluation for Invariance Testing Under Extreme Sample Imbalance and Non-Ignorable Missing Ordinal Data
ideas.description: This research develops a comprehensive Bayesian sensitivity analysis framework for measurement invariance (ME/I) testing with ordinal data. It systematically investigates how extreme group sample size ratios interact with different prior beliefs (from weakly to strongly informative) about non-ignorable missing data mechanisms to influence posterior estimates of invariance. The novel synergy lies in using the imbalance as a stress-test to identify which prior specifications robustly recover true invariance parameters and which lead to spurious findings, thereby providing practical guidelines for prior justification in realistically imbalanced and incomplete datasets.

ideas.title: Causal Diagnosis and Adaptive Correction for ME/I Testing Under Extreme Imbalance and Missing Ordinal Data
ideas.description: This research moves beyond documenting performance to explicitly identifying the causal mechanisms by which extreme sample size ratios and missing ordinal data lead to Type I/II error inflation in missingness- and ignorability (ME/I) tests. It employs a simulation-based diagnostic framework to isolate failure pathways, such as biased information matrix estimation or flawed imputation under imbalance. Based on this root-cause analysis, the study designs and validates an adaptive testing procedure that selects or combines methods (e.g., robust variance corrections, permutation adjustments, or Bayesian imputation) in response to the diagnosed data conditions, ensuring robust inference regardless of the severity of imbalance or missingness.

ideas.title: Adapting Image Inpainting Priors for Missing Data in Small-Sample SEM
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computer vision—image inpainting—to address missing data in small-sample Structural Equation Modeling (SEM). In image inpainting, missing pixels are reconstructed by propagating information from known neighboring areas, guided by priors about image structure like smoothness or edge continuity. The analogous solution here is to treat the missing data points in an SEM dataset as 'gaps' to be filled, using the model-implied relationships (the specified paths and latent variable structures) as the guiding 'structural prior.' The adapted research idea involves developing a modified RLS (Regularized Least Squares) or related estimator that performs simultaneous model estimation and data imputation in an iterative loop. In each iteration, the current parameter estimates inform plausible values for the missing data, and these imputed values, in turn, are used to re-estimate the model, with regularization ensuring stability in small samples. The process converges to a solution where the imputed values are maximally consistent with the model's theoretical structure, effectively 'inpainting' the missing data based on the relational patterns present in the observed data and the model specification, under a missing-at-random assumption.

ideas.title: Leverage-Robust SEM via Integrated Covariance Downweighting and M-Estimation
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea directly combats the vulnerability of standard robust M-estimation in SEM to high-leverage outliers. It first pre-processes the observed data using a robust multivariate method to identify and assign low weights to observations that are extreme in the indicator space, thus mitigating their undue influence on the estimated covariance matrix. This leverage-adjusted weighting scheme is then integrated into a subsequent robust M-estimation fitting stage, which downweights based on model residuals. The combined approach ensures robustness against both types of outliers, leading to more reliable parameter estimates and model fit indices even in contaminated data.

ideas.title: Benchmarking RLS against ensemble corrections for small-sample ML, inspired by climate model downscaling
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to a parallel problem in climate science, where ensembles of statistical downscaling methods are benchmarked to correct coarse global model outputs for regional use. The analogous solution involves systematic, multi-criteria evaluation of correction ensembles against high-quality reference data under diverse conditions. Adapted to the original ML idea, this means benchmarking Regularized Likelihood Solutions (RLS) not in isolation, but as part of an ensemble of small-sample corrections (e.g., scaled chi-square, bootstrap, Bartlett, Swain) for Maximum Likelihood estimation. The study will simulate a wide range of small-sample conditions (sample size, model complexity, distributional properties) and use a comprehensive set of performance metrics (bias, mean squared error, confidence interval coverage, Type I error rate, computational efficiency) to create a diagnostic benchmark. The goal is to produce a principled guideline, similar to climate downscaling intercomparison projects, that maps specific small-sample data characteristics to the most robust and efficient correction method.

ideas.title: Cross-Validated Ridge Regularization of the GLS Weight Matrix
ideas.description: This approach systematically applies ridge regularization to the estimated covariance matrix in Generalized Least Squares (GLS) to address ill-conditioning, but crucially embeds it within a model selection framework. The optimal ridge parameter λ is selected by minimizing a cross-validated criterion based on the predictive performance of the resulting GLS estimator, preventing arbitrary bias introduction. This creates a robust, data-adaptive method that stabilizes the weight matrix only when beneficial, yielding a more reliable and statistically principled estimator than the original heuristic idea.

ideas.title: Adapting Rare Species Survey Methods for Unequal Group Sizes with Missing Ordinal Data
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to study method performance in ME/I testing when groups have highly unequal sample sizes with missing ordinal data. Drawing an analogy from ecology, where scientists must reliably estimate parameters for rare species from uneven survey data with frequent non-detections, we adapt solutions from biodiversity monitoring. The analogous field uses occupancy modeling and adaptive, stratified designs to correct for imperfect detection and missing observations. The adapted idea is to treat the smaller group in the statistical test as a 'rare stratum' and develop a model-based inference framework. This framework would incorporate stratified sampling principles and model-based corrections (e.g., for the missing data mechanism) to provide robust performance even under extreme sample size ratios and missing ordinal data, much like ecological models account for uneven survey effort and false absences.
